metadata.title: Book of His Temptations
metadata.author: Othloh of St Emmeram
known_issues: Some place names have modern equivalents provided in parentheses (Obermunster/Nidernmunster, Amerbach) which were added by later editors—these are preserved as they aid identification.
Text breaks mid-sentence at column 0042C, suggesting incomplete source material
Latin syntax occasionally requires interpretive translation for natural English flow
Some theological arguments about angelic probation and human culpability are dense and may benefit from slower narration

The massive catalog of manuscript production (19 Missals + numerous other liturgical books) represents extraordinary output even for a dedicated monastic scribe and may include collaborative or supervised work, though Otloh claims personal authorship.

The Latin text shifts from third-person ('clericus') to first-person ('ego') partway through Part Two without clear transition—this appears to be Otloh's original stylistic choice as he moves from framing narrative to direct autobiography.

The 'chunk 312' historical reference to Emperor Henry and King Conrad is slightly confusing—the text likely refers to Henry III (died 1056) and the succession crisis involving young Henry IV, not Conrad II. This ambiguity is preserved as in the source.
Several book titles are given in Latin in the source but their full content/genre is not always clear from context alone (e.g., 'Liber Manualis', 'De cursu spirituali').

The exact count of books distributed is complex due to overlapping categories (original compositions vs. copied manuscripts vs. gift volumes). The totals given by Otloh himself have been preserved without editorial reconciliation.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Beginning of the Book of Temptations
chunks.latin: Incipit liber de tentatione cujusdam monachi.
chunks.english: The beginning of the book concerning the temptation of a certain monk.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Part One: The Clerk's Conversion to Monastic Life
chunks.latin: PARS PRIMA.
chunks.english: Part One.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fuit quidam clericus vitiis multis modis deditus, qui cum saepius a Domino commoneretur pro emendatione sua, conversus tandem venit ad monasticam professionem, nullis suorum amicorum scientibus.
chunks.english: There was a certain clerk given over to many kinds of vices. When he had been frequently admonished by the Lord for his correction, he finally converted and came to the monastic profession, without any of his friends knowing about it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In eo autem loco quo monachus factus est, cum plurimos diversae qualitatis homines invenisset, quosdam libros quidem gentiles, quosdam vero sacram Scripturam legentes, ipse coepit illum solummodo imitari, quos videbat divinae insistere lectioni.
chunks.english: In that place where he became a monk, though he found many men of different types—some reading pagan books, others reading sacred Scripture—he began to imitate only those whom he saw devoted to divine reading.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quanto autem frequentior erat in hujusmodi lectione, tanto magis molestias diabolicae tentationis sentiebat sibi inolescere; sed ille in Domino confidens gratiaeque Dei se jugiter committens, eo animo quo coepit, in sacra lectione perseverare studuit.
chunks.english: But the more frequently he engaged in such reading, the more he felt the troubles of diabolic temptation growing within him. Yet trusting in the Lord and continually committing himself to God's grace, he strove to persevere in sacred reading with the same spirit with which he had begun.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque post multum temporis ab hac impugnatione eriperetur, cogitans qualiter per ea quae passus erat tam ipse quam alii aedificari possent, scripsit non solum illatas tentationis molestias, sed etiam sacrae Scripturae verba, quae ex divina inspiratione sibi provenerant, ex quibus interea fraudi diabolicae respondendo utebatur pro clypeo.
chunks.english: When after much time he was freed from this assault, considering how both he and others might be edified by what he had suffered, he wrote down not only the troubles of temptation that had been brought upon him, but also the words of sacred Scripture that had come to him through divine inspiration, which he had meanwhile used as a shield in responding to diabolic deception.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic quippe incipit scribere de tentationum suarum molestia:
chunks.english: Thus indeed he begins to write about the trouble of his temptations:

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Monk Describes His Diabolic Delusions
chunks.latin: Delusiones Satanae varias vigilans ac dormiens pertuli
chunks.english: I endured various delusions of Satan both waking and sleeping.
chunks.notes: This begins the autobiographical account in first person

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quas licet universas nequeam promere, aliquas tamen memoriae insitas, prout possum, volo referre:
chunks.english: Though I cannot bring forth all of them, I wish to relate some that are fixed in my memory, as best I can.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: et reor hoc primum de fraudibus his perhibendum quod et ante monachalis vitae professionem, et postea diu perpessus sum, quod scilicet satis stolida improvidaque etiam inesset voluntas conversionis;
chunks.english: And I believe this should be testified first concerning these deceptions: both before my profession of monastic life and long afterward, I suffered the temptation that there existed a rather foolish and imprudent will even in my conversion itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quia contra Scripturam quae dicit: Omnia fac cum consilio, sine consilio parentum et amicorum quamque in maximo juventutis fervore positus subito vellem adire, nimisque foret inconsultum ut hujusmodi homo quisquam tam periculosum susciperet votum;
chunks.english: The tempter suggested that, against the Scripture which says, 'Do all things with counsel,' I wished to undertake this suddenly without the counsel of parents and friends, and while placed in the greatest fervor of youth. He argued it would be most ill-advised for someone like me to undertake such a dangerous vow.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ideoque multo melius esset ut praestolans usque ad aetatis maturioris perfectionem, tunc tandem cum se virtus ingesserit omnis, sponte pro desiderata tractem conversione.
chunks.english: Therefore it would be much better if I waited until the perfection of a more mature age, and then finally, when every virtue had established itself, I should freely undertake the desired conversion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec ergo et his similia imprimis etiam quasi compatiendo et consulendo intulit illusio diabolica.
chunks.english: These things and similar ones were brought upon me at first by diabolic delusion, as if sympathizing with me and giving me counsel.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deinde vero cum talia ad plenum voluntatis suae effectum, Dei gratia resistente, non potuisset tentator nequissimus in me obtinere, ad majoris excitatus est pugnam nequitiae.
chunks.english: But then, when the most wicked tempter could not achieve the full effect of his will in me—God's grace resisting—he was stirred up to a battle of greater wickedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam arte solita ad desperationem me pertrahere studuit, inspirans scilicet incassum, me talem velle reverti, qui tantis criminibus sum implicatus ut non solum principibus, sed etiam multitudini caeterae, ipsisque simul parentibus et cognatis exosus essem.
chunks.english: For by his usual art he strove to drag me toward desperation, suggesting that it was in vain for someone like me to want to turn back, since I was entangled in so many crimes that I was hateful not only to rulers but also to all the common people, and even to my own parents and relatives.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Putasne, inquit, ut tam sceleratus homo apud Deum judicem districtissimum veniam possit promereri? quandoquidem, sicut scriptum est: Vix justus salvabitur.
chunks.english: He said, 'Do you think that such a wicked man can merit pardon from God, the most strict judge? Especially since, as it is written, the righteous person is scarcely saved.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ne cupias ergo quae non sunt cupienda, sed potius ad ea laboris tui studium converte quae amodo possis obtinere.
chunks.english: 'Therefore do not desire things that should not be desired, but rather turn the effort of your labor toward those things that you can obtain from now on.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim ita, ut tu studiosissime aestimas, omnibus concessa foret hujusmodi facultas, ut scilicet ad regna coelestia tam perversus quam justus homo pertingere posset, nequaquam Apostolus ille excellentissimus Paulus diceret: Non enim est omnium fides.
chunks.english: 'For if, as you most earnestly suppose, such an ability were granted to all—that a perverted man could reach the kingdoms of heaven just as much as a righteous one—that most excellent Apostle Paul would certainly not say, For not all have faith.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et iterum idem dicit: Non omnes obediunt Evangelio; ipseque Salvator et veracissimus auctor haec rudimenta suis mundoque dedit omni: Qui potest capere capiat.
chunks.english: 'And again the same apostle says, Not all obey the Gospel. And the Savior himself, the most truthful author, gave this fundamental teaching to his own and to the whole world: Let him who can receive it, receive it.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In qua procul dubio sententia innotuit, quod non omnis bona possit.
chunks.english: 'In which saying it is made clear beyond doubt that not everyone can achieve good things.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo delusionibus torquerer talibus, quis putas etiam tunc erat animus? nil aliud certe tunc praevalui nisi flere, et juxta Psalmistam fuerunt lacrymae meae panes die ac nocte.
chunks.english: When I was tormented by such delusions, what do you think my state of mind was then? Certainly I could do nothing else but weep, and according to the Psalmist, my tears were my bread day and night.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fateor nimirum ex intimo corde quod nullus, nisi per gratiam Domini solam, talia potest vincere.
chunks.english: I confess indeed from my inmost heart that no one can overcome such things except through the grace of the Lord alone.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postquam autem insidiator calidissimus me ad desperationis illatae consensum nequivit pertrahere, conatus est per alia fraudis suae argumenta ad justitiae divinae blasphemiam me deflectere, non deterrendo et improperando, sed quasi condolendo et compatiendo afflictioni meae,
chunks.english: But after the most cunning plotter could not drag me to consent to the desperation he had inflicted, he tried through other arguments of his deceit to turn me aside to blasphemy against divine justice—not by deterring and reproaching me, but as if grieving over and sympathizing with my affliction.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: suggerens videlicet cordi meo hujusmodi cogitationes. O quam miserande juvenis, cujus dolorem nullus hominum dignatur attendere! Quis autem hominum vel excogitare potest tanta te jam depressum afflictione?
chunks.english: He suggested such thoughts to my heart: 'O what a pitiable young man, whose sorrow no human being deigns to attend to! What human being could even conceive that you are weighed down by such great affliction?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde nec illis debes imputare; quia quod nequeunt scire, in eo non possunt subvenire. Novit enim Dominus solus omnia. Ideoque illi soli constat imputandum, quidquid videtur ineptum et inordinatum.
chunks.english: 'Therefore you should not blame them, because they cannot help with what they cannot know. For the Lord alone knows all things. And therefore to him alone must be attributed whatever seems inept and disordered.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo sciat et possit omnia, quare non adjuvat te in tribulatione tua, qui pro ejus amore reliquisti et prius saeculum et jam diu inaestimabilem sustines cruciatum?
chunks.english: 'Since he both knows and can do all things, why does he not help you in your tribulation—you who for love of him left the world long ago and have now endured inestimable torment for a long time?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae rogo ista erit ratio districtionis, quae ad se jugiter clamantes omnibus cruciat modis?
chunks.english: 'What kind of reason, I ask, is this severity that torments in every way those who continually cry out to him?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fac quod facturus eris; depone supervacuas preces et luctus, quia ille in hac severitate, in qua hactenus erat, amodo etiam perseverat.
chunks.english: 'Do what you are going to do. Put aside useless prayers and lamentations, because he perseveres now in the same severity in which he has been until now.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam nimis stultum est ea precibus attentare, quae te scias non posse impetrare.
chunks.english: 'For it is extremely foolish to attempt through prayers those things which you know you cannot obtain.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verumtamen noli timere, quia nunquam unius potentis injustitia omnes permittit perdere.
chunks.english: 'Nevertheless, do not fear, because the injustice of one powerful being never allows all to perish.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quomodo enim posset fieri ut homo quisquam mereretur semper affligi? vel quis mortalium sine peccato valet esse ab initio usque ad finem vitae?
chunks.english: 'For how could it happen that any human being would deserve to be afflicted always? Or what mortal can be without sin from the beginning to the end of life?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: aut parvuli innocentia qualiter acquiri potest in senecta? sicut Christus docet: Nisi, inquiens, conversi fueritis et efficiamini sicut parvuli, non intrabitis in regnum coelorum.
chunks.english: 'Or how can the innocence of a little child be acquired in old age? As Christ teaches, saying, Unless you are converted and become like little children, you will not enter the kingdom of heaven.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunquid et hoc te non movet quod Ezechiel propheta comminatur dicens: Anima quae peccaverit, ipsa morietur?
chunks.english: 'Does not this also move you, what the prophet Ezekiel threatens, saying, The soul that sins, it shall die?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim omnis qui peccat morietur, profecto nullus homo salvus erit, quia nemo absque peccato inveniri potest.
chunks.english: 'For if everyone who sins will die, then certainly no human being will be saved, because no one can be found without sin.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne similiter et illam mirabilem sententiam, quam paulo post idem propheta dicit, tua mens attendit? Ait ergo: Si averterit se justus a justitia sua et fecerit iniquitatem, omnes justitiae ejus erunt in oblivionem.
chunks.english: 'Does your mind not also attend to that remarkable saying which the same prophet speaks a little later? He says, If a righteous person turns away from his righteousness and commits iniquity, all his righteous deeds will be given over to oblivion.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc est quod ille Judas proditor Domini, cum hoc unum traditionis crimen admisisset, post multimoda justitiae opera damnatus est.
chunks.english: 'This is why that Judas, the betrayer of the Lord, when he had committed this one crime of betrayal, was condemned after his many works of righteousness.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Attende adhuc et alia duo maxima hujus rationis exempla, in quibus probari et definiri potest omnis nostra sententia.
chunks.english: 'Consider still two other major examples of this reasoning, in which all our position can be proved and established.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Legitur enim in Exodi libro, quia dixerit Dominus ad Moysen: Miserebor cui voluero, et clemens ero in quem mihi placuerit;
chunks.english: 'For it is read in the book of Exodus that the Lord said to Moses, I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and I will be merciful to whom it pleases me.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: rursumque in Evangelio scriptum est, quia volens quidam discipulus ejus fieri, ac dicens: Sequar te quocunque ieris, repulsus est ab eodem Domino dicente: Vulpes foveas habent, et volucres coeli nidos, Filius autem hominis non habet ubi caput suum reclinet.
chunks.english: 'And again it is written in the Gospel that when someone wanted to become his disciple and said, I will follow you wherever you go, he was rejected by the same Lord, who said, Foxes have dens and birds of the sky have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: O indiscreta severitas! o miseranda aequitas! quae tantummodo voluntatis suae arbitrium sequitur, et ad se confugientes suscipere dedignatur.
chunks.english: 'O indiscriminate severity! O pitiable justice! That follows only the judgment of its own will and disdains to receive those who flee to it.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Intolerabilis certe talis est disciplina nullique merito placitura.
chunks.english: 'Such discipline is certainly intolerable and will deservedly please no one.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hujusmodi quoque delusiones diu perturbaverunt cogitationes meas, in quibus satis probari valet quantis periculis mens mea subjaceret.
chunks.english: Such delusions disturbed my thoughts for a long time, in which it can be sufficiently proved how many dangers my mind was subject to.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad easdem delusiones videtur pertinere quod subsequenter volo referre.
chunks.english: What I wish to relate next seems to belong to these same delusions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe namque contigit ut quia matutinis horis ad signum primum, sicut regula sancta docet, volui exsurgere, phantasmatico aliquo signo longe ante tempus surgendi excitatus, ad oratorium venirem festinus.
chunks.english: For it often happened that because I wished to rise at the first signal in the morning hours, as the holy rule teaches, I was awakened by some phantom signal long before the time of rising, and came hastily to the oratory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc etiam tandiu credidi opus esse divinum, quousque ex tempestivi soporis impedimento coactum me sentirem ad intempestivum.
chunks.english: I believed for a long time that this was a divine work, until I felt myself forced from timely sleep to untimely waking.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Patiebar et hoc per aliquot annos in nocturnis horis, ut licet admodum sanus in lectulo dormiens jacerem, cum ad matutinas laudes surgere deberem, quasi compede quodam membrorum omnium constringerer debilitate: sicque ad ecclesiam nutanti et difficillimo gressu pervenirem.
chunks.english: I also suffered this for several years during the night hours: although I lay quite healthy asleep in bed, when I should rise for morning praise, I was as if bound by some fetter of weakness in all my limbs, and thus came to church with unsteady and most difficult steps.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Temptation to Doubt God's Existence
chunks.latin: Proferenda est adhuc tentatio et delusio una
chunks.english: One more temptation and delusion must be brought forth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quam tanto difficilius intimare queo, quanto minus unquam aliquid hujusmodi legebam aut audiebam ab ullo.
chunks.english: This I can describe all the more difficultly because I had never read or heard anything of this kind from anyone.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postquam enim suprascriptis et aliis multis tentationibus (quarum quasdam minime in memoria habeo, quasdam vero, ne legentibus sim taedio, hic proferre nolo,) sum impulsus, sed per Dei gratiam a fide et spe coelestis auxilii nequaquam evulsus,
chunks.english: For after I was assailed by the above-mentioned temptations and many others—some of which I have no memory of, and some of which I do not wish to bring forth here lest I become tedious to readers—yet by God's grace I was never torn away from faith and hope of heavenly help.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: tunc impugnatione tali diutius torqueri me sentiebam, per quam et de Scripturae sacrae scientia et ipsius Dei essentia prorsus dubitare compellebar.
chunks.english: Then I felt myself tormented for a long time by such an assault through which I was compelled to doubt completely both the knowledge of sacred Scripture and the very essence of God himself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In aliarum quidem tentationum discrimine aliquid refrigerii intervallum, aliquid spei praestabatur refugium; in istis vero per horas continuas omnis fere solatii privabar cognitione.
chunks.english: In the crisis of other temptations, some interval of relief was provided, some refuge of hope was offered. But in these, for continuous hours, I was deprived of almost all knowledge of consolation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In aliis per sacrae documenta Scripturae aliquatenus roboratus, contra illata mortis jacula fidei speique armis decertavi; in ista autem omni dubitatione et mentis caecitate circumseptus, si aut ulla in Scripturis sacris veritas sit ac profectus, aut si Deus omnipotens constet prorsus dubitavi.
chunks.english: In other temptations, strengthened somewhat by the teachings of sacred Scripture, I fought with the weapons of faith and hope against the darts of death that were hurled at me. But in this one, surrounded by complete doubt and blindness of mind, I doubted utterly whether there was any truth and benefit in the sacred Scriptures, or whether almighty God even exists.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In aliis, inquam, tolerabilis aliquo modo et temperata fuit impugnationis causa; in ista autem talis erumpebat violentia ut non solum spirituales, sed etiam corporales mei sensus vigore solito destituerentur.
chunks.english: In the others, I say, the cause of the assault was in some way tolerable and tempered. But in this one such violence broke forth that not only my spiritual senses but even my bodily senses were deprived of their usual vigor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Videbatur enim mihi interdum, ut quasi obvelato visu et auditu nil juxta vires solitas videre aut audire possem.
chunks.english: For it seemed to me at times as if, with sight and hearing veiled, I could see or hear nothing according to my usual powers.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Interim ego quasi cujusdam mecum colloquentis et ore etiam apposito verba meis auribus susurrando immittentis audirem dicentisque:
chunks.english: Meanwhile I seemed to hear someone conversing with me, even placing his mouth close and whispering words into my ears, saying:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cur labore casso tandiu fatigaris? ubi est illa spes tua quam usque modo retinebas in Scriptura?
chunks.english: 'Why do you exhaust yourself so long with empty labor? Where is that hope of yours which you held until now in Scripture?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne, omnium mortalium stultissime, casibus propriis poteris probare, quia et Scripturarum testificatio, et totius creaturae imaginatio absque ratione constat et sine rectore?
chunks.english: 'You, most foolish of all mortals, cannot you prove by your own experiences that both the testimony of the Scriptures and the conception of all creation exist without reason and without a ruler?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: nunquid experimento non cognoscis quia aliud librorum relatio divinorum et aliud vita moresque probantur esse hominum?
chunks.english: 'Do you not know by experience that the account of divine books is one thing, and the life and customs of human beings are proved to be something else?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Putasne tot millia hominum errare, qui, ut ipse quoque hactenus cernebas, nec observare seu nec suscipere curant documenta librorum?
chunks.english: 'Do you think so many thousands of human beings are in error, who, as you yourself have observed until now, care neither to observe nor to receive the teachings of books?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque super his moestus in corde tractarem saepius, quasi interrogando et objiciendo: Si ita est, inquiens, cur tanta convenientia est in omnibus fere Scripturis divinitus inspiratis, ut ratione pari et de conditore Deo et de mandatorum ipsius observantia enarrent?
chunks.english: When I often pondered these things with sadness in my heart, as if questioning and objecting—saying, 'If this is so, why is there such great agreement in almost all divinely inspired Scriptures, that they speak with equal reason about God the creator and about the observance of his commandments?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Visum est hujusmodi verba iterum quasi respondendo mihi inspirari:
chunks.english: Words of this kind seemed to be inspired in me again, as if in response:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideo utique, o insensate, Scriptura in qua confidis de Dei persona et de religione multiplici verba profert quomodo homines illi qui Scripturae ejusdem auctores olim exstiterunt, eodem modo quo etiam in tempore praesenti vivunt, ipsi tunc vixerunt.
chunks.english: 'Precisely for this reason, O foolish one, the Scripture in which you trust speaks words about the person of God and about manifold religion, because those men who were once the authors of that same Scripture lived then in the same way as people also live in the present time.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Modus autem vivendi in tempore praesenti, sicut tu quoque scis, constat talis, ut honeste quidem et religiose satis homines loquantur, aliter vero longe sint actus eorum, sicut scilicet expedit et fragilitas humana permittit.
chunks.english: 'But the manner of living in the present time, as you also know, is such that people speak honorably and quite religiously, yet their actions are far otherwise, namely as expediency and human frailty permit.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne ergo haec ita esse quotidie potes probare? Unde putes et auctores scripturarum antiquarum religiosa quidem honestaque dicta composuisse, sed non secundum eorumdem dictorum qualitatem vixisse.
chunks.english: 'Can you not prove daily that these things are so? Wherefore you should think that the authors of ancient scriptures composed religious and honorable sayings indeed, but did not live according to the quality of those same sayings.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Igitur secundum talem modum omnes legis divinae libros intellige conscriptos, ut videlicet religiositatis et virtutis superficiem quamdam exterius habeant, interius vero rationem aliam et intellectum exquirant.
chunks.english: 'Therefore understand all the books of divine law to be written according to such a manner, that they have some surface of religiosity and virtue externally, but internally they seek another reason and understanding.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut in plurimis maximeque in divinis codicibus facile reperiuntur sententiae, aliam in littera, aliam in intelligentia rationem retinentes.
chunks.english: 'Just as in very many places, especially in divine books, sayings are easily found that retain one meaning in the letter and another in the understanding.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec autem omnia ita esse uno Pauli testimonio approbo: Littera enim, inquit, occidit, spiritus autem, id est sensus, vivificat.
chunks.english: 'I prove that all these things are so by one testimony of Paul. For he says, The letter kills, but the spirit—that is, the meaning—gives life.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne satis aperte per haec Apostoli verba doceris quia, si librorum dicta sequeris, maxima pericula patieris?
chunks.english: 'Are you not taught clearly enough through these words of the Apostle that if you follow the sayings of books, you will suffer the greatest dangers?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem quoque de Dei essentia intelligendum est. Alioquin si aliqua persona aut virtus Dei omnipotentis esset, nequaquam tanta confusio atque diversitas in rebus cunctis appareret.
chunks.english: 'The same must also be understood about the essence of God. Otherwise, if there were any person or power of almighty God, such great confusion and diversity would not appear in all things.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed neque tibi discrimina tanta contingerent, talisque dubitatio, quam modo pateris, minime immineret.
chunks.english: 'Nor would such great crises befall you, and such doubt as you now suffer would not threaten you at all.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque hujusmodi delusionibus plusquam credibile sit impugnarer, et propter inauditam ipsius impugnationis qualitatem ulli fratrum aperte indicare vererer (nullum enim talia posse credere aut audire arbitrabar);
chunks.english: When I was assailed by such delusions—more than one would believe possible—and because of the unheard-of nature of the assault itself I feared to reveal it openly to any of the brothers, for I thought no one could believe or bear to hear such things.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Prayer of Desperation and Divine Response
chunks.latin: tunc solo prostratus, et prae amaritudine suspiria longa trahens et vires animi colligens, emisi talem labiis et pectore vocem
chunks.english: Then prostrate on the ground, drawing long sighs from bitterness and gathering the strength of my spirit, I sent forth such a voice from lips and heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: O si quis es, Omnipotens, et si sis undique praesens, sicut et in libris legi saepissime multis, jam precor ostende quis sis et quid possis, eripiens citius me a periculis imminentibus; nam sufferre magis nequeo discrimina tanta.
chunks.english: 'O if you are someone, Almighty One, and if you are present everywhere, as I have read very often in many books, now I beg you, show who you are and what you can do, rescuing me quickly from these imminent dangers. For I can no longer bear such great crises.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nulla dehinc mora: et ablata est per Dei gratiam non solum omnis illa dubitationis supradictae nebula, sed etiam scientiae lux tanta in corde meo emicuit ut et nunquam postmodum tales dubitationis mortiferae tenebras sustinerem, et ea quae minus antea cognovi intelligere coepissem.
chunks.english: There was no delay thereafter. By God's grace not only was all that fog of the aforementioned doubt taken away, but also such a great light of knowledge shone forth in my heart that never afterward did I endure such darkness of deadly doubt, and I began to understand things I had known less before.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cujus etiam intelligentiae gratia in tantum per idem tempus mihi augebatur, ut non eam facile possem occultare.
chunks.english: The grace of this understanding was so increased for me during that same time that I could not easily conceal it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Instinctu enim quodam ineffabili et fervore insolito succensus, aliquam laudis divinae operam pro gratia collata assumere incitabar.
chunks.english: For kindled by some ineffable impulse and unusual fervor, I was stirred to undertake some work of divine praise for the grace bestowed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque eamdem intelligentiae gratiam nec prorsus tacere nec convenienti ratione possem perhibere, tractare coepi, quia forsitan dictando et scribendo fervor insitus liceret proferri.
chunks.english: Since I could neither remain completely silent about this same grace of understanding, nor bear witness to it in a fitting manner, I began to consider whether perhaps the fervor within me might be allowed to come forth through dictation and writing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc itaque modo occasionem scribendi sumens, et ea quae superius de illusione diabolica proferebam, et quae adhuc proferre volo, de divinae inspirationis colloquio scripsi, ita incipiens.
chunks.english: Taking the occasion for writing in this way, I wrote both what I brought forth above about diabolic delusion and what I still wish to bring forth, from the discourse of divine inspiration, beginning thus:

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Divine Inspiration: God's Response to the Temptations
chunks.latin: Quoniam igitur delusiones diabolicas peccatorum meorum causa exortas atque contra me suscitatas aliquantulum protuli
chunks.english: Since therefore I have brought forth to some extent the diabolic delusions that arose because of my sins and were stirred up against me.
chunks.notes: This begins the section of divine responses

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: consequens profecto et rationabile videtur, ut etiam divinae inspirationis modos, quibus mens mea ad repugnandum instruebatur, scribendo aperiam,
chunks.english: It seems indeed consequent and reasonable that I should also reveal in writing the modes of divine inspiration by which my mind was instructed for resistance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ne forte aliquis delusiones easdem hic tantummodo scriptas, et non protinus coelestis adjutorii instrumenta ex lectione pari agnoscens; aut me victoriam adeptam, quae mea nunquam est, mihimet deputasse arbitretur;
chunks.english: This I do lest anyone, recognizing these delusions as written here alone and not immediately recognizing from equal reading the instruments of heavenly help, should think that I have attributed to myself the victory achieved, which is never mine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: seu etiam, quasi mihi defuisset protectio divina in meis, in suis pariter tentationibus idem sibi venturum vereatur.
chunks.english: Or lest anyone should fear that the same thing will come to him in his own temptations, as if divine protection had been lacking to me in mine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cui suspicioni nullum dans locum, fateor me contra diaboli insidias omni modo per gratiam divinam instructum, seu desidia lasciviaque animi torpentem nullo unquam certamine debito, aut vigiliarum, aut jejunii, seu cujuscunque continentiae pro viribus datis pugnasse.
chunks.english: Giving no place to this suspicion, I confess that although I was instructed in every way by divine grace against the snares of the devil, I never fought with any due struggle of vigils or fasting or any continence according to the powers given—languishing as I was in sloth and laxity of spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam ut praeteream collata scientiae liberalis dona quae circumspectionis instrumenta constant maxima, memini me frequenter, et maxime cum primum de stratu meo in matutinis exsurgerem horis,
chunks.english: For to pass over the gifts of liberal knowledge that had been bestowed, which are the greatest instruments of discernment, I remember that frequently, and especially when I first rose from my bed in the morning hours,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: mox a quodam quasi mecum surgente, mecumque simul gradiente, per ineffabilem modum nunc increpari, interdum leniter admoneri, crebro etiam humillimo nisu obsecrari pro emendatione morum et vitiorum,
chunks.english: I was immediately, in an ineffable manner, sometimes rebuked, sometimes gently admonished, and often beseeched with the most humble effort for the correction of my morals and vices by someone as if rising with me and walking with me at the same time.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: eorum quidem inprimis quae hesterno forsitan die ignoranter committens pro nihilo duxi; deinde vero pro eorum emendatione, quae in quocunque tempore admisi, aut inepte ridendo, aut incaute loquendo, seu etiam inutilia cogitando:
chunks.english: This concerned first those things which perhaps the day before I had ignorantly committed and considered as nothing; then for the correction of those things which I had committed at any time, either by foolish laughing or by careless speaking, or even by thinking useless things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: postremo pro emendatione cujuslibet vitii, quod longe jam antea perpetratum vix memini.
chunks.english: Finally, for the correction of any vice which I had committed long before and scarcely remembered.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque hujusmodi inspiratione et instinctu circumventus ecclesiam intrassem, et ad orationis studium me suppliciter inclinassem aut prostravissem,
chunks.english: When, surrounded by such inspiration and impulse, I had entered the church and had humbly bowed or prostrated myself for the practice of prayer,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deus scit quod non mentior, videbantur mihi quod aliquid a me deposceret eodem studio orandi, dicens:
chunks.english: God knows I do not lie—it seemed to me that something was asking of me with that same earnestness of prayer, saying:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicuti igitur tibi gratum est ut a me rogata consequaris, ita quoque mihi pretiosum constat, si tu precibus obedias meis.
chunks.english: 'Just as it is pleasing to you to obtain from me the things you ask, so also it is precious to me if you obey my prayers.'
chunks.notes: Text breaks here mid-discourse at column 0042C

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: liberi arbitrii nisu inhaerere, et in quo omnia habere posset, ad eum postmodum lapsus cum sudore maximo poenitentiae rediret atque ejusdem arbitrii ingeniique vires, quas ante transgressionem absque sensus torpore ad cuncta bonitatis jura retinuerat, postea carnis fragilitate praepediente cum labore gravi obtineret.
chunks.english: Adam had the strength of free will to cling to me, and in me he could possess everything. Then, after his fall, he had to return to me with the great sweat of penance. The powers of will and understanding that he had before his transgression—which he held without any sluggishness of sense for all the rights of goodness—afterward, with the frailty of flesh hindering him, he could only maintain through heavy labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo hac ratiocinatione agnoscas non solum eumdem primum hominem singulari conditionis humanae primatu sapientiaque laureatum, sed etiam omnes coelicolas, quorum excellentiae creatura nulla comparatur, absque probatione congrua non posse mihi firmiter inhaerere nec non sui meique differentiam pervidere: quem deinceps hominum caeterorum a primi hominis prudentia insita aliquid distantium arbitraris esse tam perfectum, ut sine tentatione meae magnificentiae suaeque fragilitatis certum capere possit experimentum?
chunks.english: When you understand through this reasoning that not only the first man—crowned with the unique primacy of human nature and with wisdom—but also all the heavenly beings, whose excellence no creature can match, cannot firmly cling to me without proper testing, nor can they clearly see the difference between themselves and me: which of the remaining humans, who fall short of the inborn prudence of the first man, do you think is so perfect that without temptation he could gain certain experience of my magnificence and his own fragility?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod si tentationis insolitae molestias super te irruisse causaris, attende quoque quia tu pene insolito more gratiam meam huc usque magis quam alii plures adeptus sis.
chunks.english: But if you complain that unusual troubles of temptation have rushed upon you, consider also that you have received my grace up to this point in an almost unusual measure, more than many others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne tibi insolitum videtur, a pueritia usque in praesens omnimodis te sceleribus implicatum, non solum poenas condignas evasisse, sed etiam quasi vice versa haec quae multis denegata sunt religiosis, et (quod non absque admiratione magna pensari valet) cum labore magno exquirentibus, ea nisu facillimo dona pietatis meae ingentia percepisse?
chunks.english: Doesn't it seem unusual to you that from boyhood to the present, though you were entangled in crimes of every kind, you not only escaped fitting punishments, but also—almost in reverse—received with the easiest effort those great gifts of my mercy that have been denied to many religious persons, even those seeking them with great labor (which can only be considered with great wonder)?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recordare, quaeso, quid tu absque labore solito beneficiorum, et quid caeteri plures mei electi experirentur angustiarum, priusquam pacem et requiem ullam, non dico futurae sed praesentis vitae, obtinerent, quamvis nonnulli inter beneficia sibimet collata afflictiones varias perferrent.
chunks.english: Remember, I ask you, what benefits you gained without the usual labor, and what troubles many others of my chosen ones endured before they obtained any peace and rest—I'm not even speaking of the future life, but of the present one—even though some bore various afflictions amid the benefits given to them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et ut haec apertius tuo innotescant animo, quosdam qui hujusmodi vitam ducebant, tam in Veteri quam in Novo Testamento per nomina recitare cupio.
chunks.english: And so that these things may become clearer to your mind, I want to name certain individuals who led such a life, both in the Old and New Testaments.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scisne quantos labores et tribulationes Jacob patriarcha pertulerit, priusquam ullam benedictionis perceptae a patre suo Isaac gratiam promereri potuerit?
chunks.english: Do you know how many labors and tribulations the patriarch Jacob endured before he could earn any grace from the blessing received from his father Isaac?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: nam, ut Genesis liber testatur, oderat semper Esau Jacob pro benedictione qua benedixerat ei pater; ideoque, matre suadente, idem Jacob propter fratris metum profectus est in exsilium, ubi avunculo suo 14 annos serviens, ignobilis personae sortitus est negotium.
chunks.english: For as the book of Genesis testifies, Esau always hated Jacob because of the blessing their father had given him. And so, at his mother's urging, Jacob went into exile out of fear of his brother, where he served his uncle for fourteen years, receiving the work of a lowly person.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea vero cum rediret ad patriam, in tantum adhuc fratris expavit iram, ut pro reconciliatione nuntios praemittens, non solum jam fratrem, sed et dominum appellaret, dicens: Sic loquimini domino meo Esau: Haec dicit frater tuus: Mitto nunc legationem ad dominum meum, ut inveniam gratiam in conspectu tuo.
chunks.english: Later, when he returned to his homeland, he still so feared his brother's anger that, sending messengers ahead for reconciliation, he addressed him not only as brother but as lord, saying: 'Speak thus to my lord Esau: Your brother says this: I now send this embassy to my lord, that I may find favor in your sight.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum autem advenientem fratrem procul vidisset, prae timore in terram cadens septies adoravit donec appropinquaret.
chunks.english: And when he saw his brother coming from afar, falling to the ground in fear, he bowed seven times until his brother approached.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Videsne ergo quantas afflictiones ille tantus vir sustinuerit, antequam gaudia aliqua benedictionis promissae gustaverit? At si gustavit, magnus timor haec superavit.
chunks.english: Do you see, then, how many afflictions that great man endured before he tasted any joys of the promised blessing? And even when he did taste them, great fear overshadowed those joys.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Eadem quoque de filio suo Joseph proferri possunt; quem cum somnia patefacta fratres sui magnum quemdam sibique praeponendum arbitrarentur, mox obliti totius fraternitatis, contra eum omnimoda succensi sunt invidia, adeo ut vix ab illius interfectione sese cohibentes venundarent eum Ismaelitis:
chunks.english: The same can be said about his son Joseph. When his brothers learned of his dreams and judged that he would become someone great and be set over them, immediately forgetting all brotherhood, they were inflamed with envy against him in every way. So much so that, barely restraining themselves from killing him, they sold him to the Ishmaelites.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: a quibus translatus in Aegyptum, tantam ibi contritionem pressuramque sustinuit, ut sicut de eo Psalmista commemorat, ferrum pertransiret animam ejus, donec veniret verbum ejus, id est, antequam perciperet praedestinatam et revelatam potestatem, quam non solum super omni Aegypto, sed etiam super cunctis fratribus suis ad se pro inopia confugientibus possessurus erat.
chunks.english: Transferred by them to Egypt, he endured such great crushing and oppression there that, as the Psalmist recalls about him, iron pierced his soul until his word came—that is, until he received the predestined and revealed power that he would possess not only over all Egypt, but also over all his brothers when they fled to him because of famine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Similia etiam invenire vales de praecipuo viro Moyse, et de famulo meo David, et de sancto Daniele: qui omnes, licet disparibus modis, ante et post susceptam dignitatem tribulationes periculaque maxima pertulerunt.
chunks.english: You can find similar examples in the outstanding man Moses, in my servant David, and in holy Daniel. All of them, though in different ways, endured very great tribulations and dangers both before and after they received their dignity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tales etiam viri multi inveniuntur in Veteri, sed multo plures in Testamento Novo, qui tanto majora passi sunt discrimina, quanto non jam praesentia ulla, sed tantummodo futura et aeterna desiderabant praemia.
chunks.english: Many such men are found in the Old Testament, but far more in the New Testament, who suffered all the greater dangers precisely because they no longer desired any present rewards, but only future and eternal ones.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illi namque qui ante legem aut sub lege religiose vixerunt, licet justi sanctique essent, magis tamen ab aliis quam a semetipsis illatas tribulationes sustinuerunt;
chunks.english: Those who lived religiously before the law or under the law, though they were just and holy, endured tribulations inflicted more by others than by themselves.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: isti autem quibus exortus est verus justitiae Sol, id est Christus, qui dixit: Si quis vult post me venire, abneget semetipsum, et tollat crucem suam et sequatur me; et iterum: Qui amat animam suam, perdet eam;
chunks.english: But those for whom the true Sun of justice arose—that is, Christ, who said: 'If anyone wishes to come after me, let him deny himself, take up his cross, and follow me,' and again: 'Whoever loves his life will lose it'—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: hujusmodi rudimentis incitati semetipsos offerebant tortoribus; et si tortor alius defuisset, sese cum vitiis et concupiscentiis semper crucifigebant.
chunks.english: urged on by such teachings, they offered themselves to torturers. And if no other torturer was available, they always crucified themselves along with their vices and desires.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideoque non facile terrenam sortiti sunt dignitatem, sed jugiter intendebant ad spiritualem: ut videlicet ipsi semet contra diabolicas tentationes fortiter munirent, aliosque exemplis suis erudirent.
chunks.english: And so they did not easily obtain earthly dignity, but continually strove for spiritual dignity—so that they might strongly fortify themselves against diabolical temptations and instruct others by their examples.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verumtamen priusquam passionum propriarum victoria et pace fruerentur, aut aliquibus regendis praeponerentur, plurima pertulerunt pericula, multa transierunt tempora.
chunks.english: Nevertheless, before they enjoyed victory and peace over their own passions, or were set over others to govern them, they endured very many dangers and passed through long periods of time.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quidam etiam in spirituali dignitate jam constituti, praeter afflictiones spontaneas, aegritudinis aut persecutionis variae flagella ob humilitatis custodiam a me transmissa, usque ad finem vitae praesentis sunt passi.
chunks.english: Some, even when already established in spiritual dignity, besides voluntary afflictions, also suffered scourges of sickness or various persecutions sent by me for the safeguarding of humility, right up to the end of this present life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis ergo hujusmodi viros innumerabiles, ut supradictum est, in Testamento Novo possis invenire, aliquos tamen exempli gratia nominare volo ut, cum hos in testimonium sumpsero, facilius agnoscas qualitatem caeterorum.
chunks.english: Although you can find innumerable such men in the New Testament, as mentioned above, I want to name a few for the sake of example, so that when I have presented these as witnesses, you may more easily recognize the character of the others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primis itaque apostolos Domini Jesu Christi attende quid laboraverint, quid pertulerint antequam aliquem laboris sui fructum, antequam sublimitatis percipiendae gradum, non dico in futura, sed in praesenti tantum vita, ubi Ecclesiae universae praeferendi erant, possederint.
chunks.english: First, then, consider the apostles of the Lord Jesus Christ—what they labored for, what they endured before they possessed any fruit of their labor, before they reached the degree of sublimity to be received—I'm not speaking of the future life, but only in the present life, where they were to be set over the universal Church.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dicit enim, ut evangelica verba testantur, ipse primus apostolorum Simon Petrus ad Jesum: Ecce nos reliquimus omnia, et secuti sumus te, quid ergo erit nobis?
chunks.english: For as the words of the Gospel testify, Simon Peter himself, the first of the apostles, said to Jesus: 'Behold, we have left everything and followed you. What then will there be for us?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid autem in his verbis, ubi beatus Petrus se suosque coapostolos omnia reliquisse dicit, intelligendum putas, nisi (quod maximae difficultatis est) parentes, propinquos, dulcia matrimonii ura, filios, praedia, domos, postremo voluntatem propriam omniaque carnis desideria?
chunks.english: What do you think should be understood in these words, where blessed Peter says that he and his fellow apostles have left everything, if not—which is of the greatest difficulty—parents, relatives, the sweet bonds of marriage, children, estates, homes, and finally their own will and all desires of the flesh?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quam ob rem licet apostoli, relictis omnibus suis, Dominum Jesum comitarentur jugiter et mente fideli, manifestum est tamen eosdem pro rerum suarum dimissione, nec non pro humanae fragilitatis occasione diutius a diabolo tentari et fatigari, maxime cum et ipse Dominus tentationes ejus voluerit pati.
chunks.english: For this reason, although the apostles, having left all their possessions, constantly accompanied the Lord Jesus with faithful minds, it is nevertheless clear that they were tempted and wearied by the devil for a long time because of the abandonment of their possessions and because of the occasions of human frailty—especially since the Lord himself willed to suffer his temptations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde ad eosdem apostolos dicit: Vos estis qui permansistis mecum in tentationibus meis.
chunks.english: Hence he says to these same apostles: 'You are those who have remained with me in my temptations.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea tribulatione premebantur maxima, cum ipsum Dominum, in quo omnem spem suam ponebant, comprehendi ab hominibus pessimis, et flagellari morteque turpissima condemnari videbant.
chunks.english: Moreover, they were pressed by the greatest tribulation when they saw the Lord himself, in whom they placed all their hope, seized by the most wicked men, scourged, and condemned to a most shameful death.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Huic autem tribulatione successit et altera, quia scilicet propter inusitatam sibique invisam tanti Magistri passionem, licet argumentis multis patefactam, vix ejus credebant resurrectionem.
chunks.english: Another tribulation followed this one, because on account of the unprecedented and hateful passion of so great a Teacher—though revealed by many proofs—they scarcely believed in his resurrection.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inter haec quoque nihilominus persecutione ac metu Judaeorum afficiebantur.
chunks.english: Amid these things they were also afflicted by persecution and fear of the Jews.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tantis igitur tribulationibus apostoli sunt afflicti nec non probati, priusquam in illo quo ponendi erant totius Ecclesiae fundamento collocarentur, priusquam ullum laboris sui fructum aut potestatem in animabus lucrandis meruissent adipisci.
chunks.english: Therefore, the apostles were afflicted and tested by so many tribulations before they were placed in that foundation of the whole Church where they were to be set, before they merited to obtain any fruit of their labor or power in winning souls.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed his generaliter de apostolis omnibus prolatis, nunc libet de aliquo illorum caeterorumque Patrum specialiter, exempli gratia, mentionem facere, quid uniuscujusque eorum tribulationis et angustiae ante aut post perceptam spiritualis gratiae sublimitatem pertulerit;
chunks.english: But having spoken generally about all the apostles, now I wish to make special mention, for the sake of example, of some of them and of the other Fathers—what tribulation and distress each of them endured before or after receiving the sublimity of spiritual grace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ut cum tam praecipuos Patres innumeris tribulationibus afflictos agnoveris, insolitam magis gratiam quam molestiam ullius tentationis hactenus esse tibi collatam probare possis.
chunks.english: So that when you recognize that such outstanding Fathers were afflicted with innumerable tribulations, you may prove that what has been given to you up to now is unusual grace rather than the trouble of any temptation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Attende ergo ad sanctum Paulum et considera quot perpessus fuerit genera persecutionum.
chunks.english: Consider therefore holy Paul and think about how many kinds of persecutions he endured.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam sicut in libro Actuum apostolorum et in ipsius Epistolis valet agnosci, postquam Christo Jesu vocante ad conversionem venit, nunquam fere sine adversantium persequentiumque periculis exstitit.
chunks.english: For as can be known from the book of Acts of the Apostles and from his own letters, after he came to conversion at Christ Jesus' calling, he was almost never without the dangers of opponents and persecutors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ejus quoque collegam in persecutionum duntaxat numerositate Athanasium Alexandrinae Ecclesiae praesulem accipe, qui, sicut in Historia ecclesiastica legitur, mox post susceptam praesulatus dignitatem, pro catholicae fidei defensione laborans immensis haereticorum persequentium insidiis pene usque ad vitae suae finem afficiebatur.
chunks.english: Take also as his colleague—at least in the multitude of persecutions—Athanasius, bishop of the Church of Alexandria. As is read in the Ecclesiastical History, immediately after receiving the dignity of bishop, laboring for the defense of the Catholic faith, he was afflicted by the immense plots of persecuting heretics almost until the end of his life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic quippe de eo sacra Scriptura refert: Hujus, inquit, tanti in Ecclesia pro fidei integritate agones fuerunt ut etiam de hoc dictum esse videatur, quod scriptum est: Ego enim ostendam ei quanta oporteat eum pati pro nomine meo.
chunks.english: For sacred Scripture reports of him: 'So great were his struggles in the Church for the integrity of the faith that it seems that what is written was said about him: For I will show him how much he must suffer for my name.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In hujus namque persecutionem universus conjuravit orbis, et commoti sunt principes terrae, gentes, regna, exercitus convenerunt adversus eum.
chunks.english: For the whole world conspired in his persecution, and the princes of the earth were stirred up, peoples, kingdoms, and armies came together against him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille autem divinum illud conferebat eloquium dicens: Si consistant adversum me castra, non timebit cor meum; si exsurgat praelium, in hoc ego sperabo.
chunks.english: But he applied to himself that divine saying: 'Though armies encamp against me, my heart will not fear; though war arise against me, in this I will hope.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isti igitur duo viri in exemplum humanae persecutionis sufficiant tibi, ut non solum nihil insolitum, nihil inauditum te aliquando ab homine quoquam pertulisse arbitreris, sed nec particulam minimam earum persecutionum quas alii pertulerunt te passum esse noveris.
chunks.english: Let these two men suffice for you as examples of human persecution, so that you may judge not only that you have never endured anything unusual or unheard of from any person, but also that you know you have not suffered even the smallest fraction of the persecutions that others endured.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tu enim pro eo quod aliquantulum adversitatis et persecutionis prius in saeculo constitutus pertulisti, et nunc in monasterio positus parvissimis tentationum stimulis, quibus etiam Christianus omnis probandus erit, fatigaris; majoribus quam alii persecutionum tentationumque periculis afflictum te frequenter arbitraris;
chunks.english: For you—because you endured a little adversity and persecution earlier when you were in the world, and now placed in the monastery you are wearied by the smallest stings of temptations, by which every Christian must be tested—you frequently think yourself afflicted by greater dangers of persecutions and temptations than others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: sed haec longe aliter quam tibi videantur invenies, si veritatem consideres.
chunks.english: But you will find these things to be far different from how they seem to you, if you consider the truth.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam quod in persecutionibus violentia humana illatis nil simile aliis pluribus pertuleris, jam satis demonstratum est exemplis.
chunks.english: For the fact that in persecutions inflicted by human violence you have endured nothing similar to many others has already been sufficiently demonstrated by examples.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deinde vero oportet considerare quantum aliis in afflictione carnis et in omni contemptu vitae hujus sponte laborantibus succumbas.
chunks.english: Next, however, you must consider how much you fall short of others who voluntarily labor in affliction of the flesh and in all contempt of this life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Attende itaque in duos venerandos Patres et spiritualis vitae doctores, sanctum videlicet Antonium ac beatum Benedictum, quorum tanto facilius potes reeordari, quanto magis ex lectione assidua tibi sunt noti.
chunks.english: Consider therefore two venerable Fathers and teachers of the spiritual life—namely, holy Antony and blessed Benedict—whom you can more easily recall because they are better known to you from constant reading.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illorum ergo vitam legens, cito potes cognoscere quantam uterque pertulerit afflictionem pro Dei amore, quam devote, inprimis cum adhuc juvenilis essent aetatis, mundana reliquerunt omnia; quantas deinde diaboli sustinuerint insidias et tentamenta.
chunks.english: Reading their lives, you can quickly learn how much affliction each endured for the love of God, how devoutly they abandoned all worldly things—especially when they were still of youthful age—and then how many plots and temptations of the devil they sustained.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unus namque eorum, id est beatus Antonius, a quo etiam omnis fere exorta est eremitarum et coenobitarum institutio, tanta conversationis sanctitate pollere coepit, ut ex hoc antiquus hostis invidiae facibus omnimodis accensus non solum spiritualibus et invisibilibus eum impugnaret insidiis, verum etiam corporalibus atque apertis afficeret plagis.
chunks.english: For one of them, that is, blessed Antony—from whom arose almost all the institution of hermits and cenobites—began to excel in such great holiness of life that the ancient enemy, inflamed in every way by the torches of envy, not only attacked him with spiritual and invisible plots but even afflicted him with bodily and visible blows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et alter nihilominus Dei gratia repletus, maxima sanctitatis suae monimenta tam scriptis quam moribus reliquit.
chunks.english: But the other, filled no less with God's grace, left the greatest monuments of his holiness both in writings and in conduct.
chunks.notes: Referring to Benedict of Nursia and his Rule

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid multa? Quidquid afflictionis, quidquid constat laboris in vigiliis et in jejuniis, aut in solitudinis horrore, et in pretiosi vestimenti abjectione, seu in desideriorum carnalium devincenda passione, aut in omnigenis invidiae diabolicae dolis superandis, eosdem invenies diu tolerasse, antequam ad arcem perfectionis qua plurimis praeferendi erant, pervenirent.
chunks.english: What more can I say? Whatever affliction, whatever labor there is in vigils and fasts, or in the horror of solitude, in the rejection of costly clothing, or in overcoming the passion of carnal desires, or in conquering all kinds of diabolic envy's tricks—you will find that they endured these things for a long time before they reached the height of perfection by which they were to be preferred to many.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec igitur, quaeso, diligenter adverte, et tantas Patrum praenominatorum afflictiones ad tuas, quas tu interdum insolitas arbitraris, confer tribulationes: et tunc procul dubio cognosces quia parvi sunt quos modo aut unquam pertulisti dolores, quantumvis iidem Patres nullatenus, sicut tu, pro eriminum capitalium poenitentia mererentur affligi.
chunks.english: Therefore, I ask you, carefully consider these things and compare the great afflictions of the aforementioned Fathers to your tribulations, which you sometimes judge to be unusual. Then without doubt you will know that the sorrows you now endure or have ever endured are small, even though those same Fathers in no way deserved, as you do, to be afflicted for penance for capital crimes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea tibi in aetate primaeva multo majora quam illis humani solatii adjumenta; multoque plura quam illis impensa sunt a me exterioris hominis solatia: quibus jure incitatus, fide atque devotione magna properare debuisses ad obtinendam interioris hominis puritatem, quae non nisi per magnam carnis acquiritur afflictionem.
chunks.english: Moreover, in your early age you had much greater helps of human consolation than they did, and many more consolations of the outer man were bestowed on you by me than on them. Justly urged by these, you should have hastened with great faith and devotion to obtain the purity of the inner man, which is acquired only through great affliction of the flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim illi beneficiis parvis adjuti ita sunt inflammati, ut labores maximos pro consequenda vitae spiritalis puritate sustinerent, cur tibi, qui parvus adhuc dona adeptus es ingentia, videtur grave tribulationem saltem aliquam pro puritate eadem tolerare?
chunks.english: For if they, helped by small benefits, were so inflamed that they sustained the greatest labors to attain the purity of spiritual life, why does it seem burdensome to you—who though still small have obtained great gifts—to endure at least some tribulation for that same purity?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dic, rogo, quis tibi videris, ut nec pro subsidiis praesentibus, nec pro futura et perenni mercede molestiam ullam perferre velis?
chunks.english: Tell me, I ask, who do you think you are, that you are unwilling to bear any trouble either for present helps or for future and everlasting reward?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid ergo olim, cum in schola positus esses, pro capacitate magna discendi laborasti? Quod deinde in saeculari vita positus habuisti studium, ut ejus inanem noxiumque evaderes luxum?
chunks.english: What then? Formerly, when you were placed in school, how you labored for great capacity in learning! Then what zeal did you have when placed in secular life to escape its vain and harmful luxury?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid etiam nunc cum ad monasterium venires, jejunando, vigilando, orando, seu in qualicunque disciplina regulari insistendo plus aliis laboras, ut et pro praeteritis criminibus consequaris veniam, et de futuris habeas cautelam?
chunks.english: And what even now, when you came to the monastery—by fasting, keeping vigil, praying, or by persevering in whatever regular discipline—do you labor more than others so that you may obtain pardon for past crimes and have caution regarding future ones?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Denique qui praeterita rite emendare, et futura mala cupit devitare, necesse est ut in vigiliis, jejuniis et orationibus nec non in aliis bonis operibus carnem studeat edomare.
chunks.english: After all, whoever wishes to amend the past properly and avoid future evils must necessarily strive to subdue the flesh in vigils, fasts, and prayers, as well as in other good works.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed tamen quantum conversionis novitas et laboris tui permittit exiguitas, curam assiduam tibi inesse pro his omnibus assequendis non denego.
chunks.english: But nevertheless, insofar as the newness of your conversion and the smallness of your labor permit, I do not deny that you have constant care for attaining all these things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et ego eadem pietatis meae beneficia, quae hactenus tibimet exhibui, amodo non auferam a te; tu tantum viriliter age, et confortetur cor tuum, et sustine Dominum.
chunks.english: Therefore I will not take away from you henceforth the same benefits of my mercy that I have shown you up to now. Only act manfully, and let your heart be strengthened, and wait for the Lord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adhuc restat afflictio et tentatio una, id est passio concupiscentiae carnalis diuturna, in qua quia te specialiter vexatum esse credis, ideoque in hac assumenda sunt aliquorum Patrum exempla ut in caeteris.
chunks.english: There still remains one affliction and temptation—that is, the long-lasting passion of carnal concupiscence, in which you believe yourself to be especially tormented. And so in this matter, as in the others, the examples of certain Fathers must be presented.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed hoc in primis pensandum est quia is qui nunquam, et qui saepius carnis corruptionem expertus est, nulla possunt colluctatione coaequari.
chunks.english: But this must be considered first: that he who has never experienced the corruption of the flesh and he who has experienced it often cannot be equaled in any struggle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis enim uterque impugnatione gravi affligatur, is tamen qui hujusmodi viro fragilitate humana devictus saepe succubuit, tanto longiora et graviora passionum cardinalium certamina sustinebit, quanto frequentius eisdem passionibus consentiens semet subjecit.
chunks.english: For although both are afflicted by grave assault, he who has often succumbed to this kind of vice, conquered by human frailty, will sustain much longer and more severe battles of the cardinal passions, the more frequently he subjected himself by consenting to those same passions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hac igitur re considerata, aliquos jam utriusque qualitatis homines ex Scriptura sacra probatos ad exemplum assume, ut perspecta aliorum tentatione atque labore facilius impugnationis et laboris tui quantitatem agnoscas.
chunks.english: Having considered this matter, then, take as examples some men of both kinds approved from sacred Scripture, so that having observed the temptation and labor of others, you may more easily recognize the extent of your own assault and labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et primum quidem illi qui pro castitatis intimae perseverantia, deinde vero hi qui pro corruptionis luxuriosaeque vitae poenitentia agenda certaverunt, proponantur.
chunks.english: And let those be proposed first who struggled for the perseverance of inner chastity, and then those who struggled to do penance for a life of corruption and luxury.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut ergo Dialogorum liber narrat, sanctus Equitius cum in juventutis suae tempore nimio carnalium desideriorum certamine fuisset fatigatus, ipsis suae tentationis angustiis ad orationis studium solertior est factus.
chunks.english: As the book of Dialogues narrates, holy Equitius, when in the time of his youth he was wearied by excessive struggle with carnal desires, was made more diligent in the practice of prayer by the very distresses of his temptation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque hac in re ab omnipotente Deo remedium continuis precibus quaereret, nocte quadam assistente angelo, eunuchizari se vidit.
chunks.english: And when he sought a remedy for this matter from almighty God with continuous prayers, one night, with an angel present, he saw himself made a eunuch.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce quantum sanctus iste laboravit, qui quanto magis impugnabatur, tanto studiosius oravit ne vinceretur.
chunks.english: See how much this saint labored—the more he was assailed, the more zealously he prayed not to be overcome.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In collationibus quoque Patrum de quodam abbate eximio, qui dictus est Serenus, legitur quia, cum pro interna corporis et animae castitate nocturnis diurnisque precibus, jejuniis, etiam ac vigiliis infatigabiliter insisteret,
chunks.english: Also, in the Conferences of the Fathers, it is read concerning a certain outstanding abbot who was called Serenus that, when he persisted untiringly in nightly and daily prayers, fasts, and vigils for the internal chastity of body and soul,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: adveniens ad eum in visione nocturna angelus Domini ejusque velut aperiens uterum, quamdam ignitam carnis strumam de ejus visceribus avellens atque projiciens, suisque omnia, ut fuerant, locis intestina restituens: «Ecce, inquit, incentiva carnis tuae abscissa, et obtinuisse te noveris hodierna die perpetuam corporis puritatem quam fideliter poposcisti.»
chunks.english: an angel of the Lord came to him in a vision at night and, as if opening his belly, tearing out and casting away a certain fiery swelling of the flesh from his internal organs, and restoring all the intestines to their places as they had been, said: 'Behold, the incentives of your flesh are cut away, and know that you have obtained today the perpetual purity of body that you faithfully sought.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce vides quantos sit passus et iste labores. Quapropter ut nemo excusationem aut diffidentiam habeat ea quae fideliter poscit impetrare non posse, quod uterque constanter petiit assequi meruit.
chunks.english: See, you observe how many labors this man also suffered. Therefore, so that no one may have excuse or diffidence that he cannot obtain what he faithfully seeks, each of these merited to attain what he steadfastly sought.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et ut hujusmodi exempla non solum in fortiori, sed etiam in sexu habeas infirmiori, in Vita Patrum narratur quoniam abbatissa quaedam sancta, nomine Sara, 13 annis fortiter a fornicationis daemone sit impugnata, et nunquam rogaverit ut discederet ab ea hujusmodi pugna, sed hoc solum dixerit: «Da mihi fortitudinem, Deus.»
chunks.english: And so that you may have such examples not only in the stronger sex but also in the weaker, in the Lives of the Fathers it is narrated that a certain holy abbess named Sarah was strongly assailed by the demon of fornication for thirteen years, and never asked that such a struggle depart from her, but said only this: 'Give me strength, O God.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce fides qualis et quantus amor pietatis; ecce quid uterque sexus laboraverit pro virtute castitatis.
chunks.english: Behold what faith and how great a love of piety! Behold what each sex has labored for the virtue of chastity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De quorum omnium puritatis constantia sicut procul es effectus, ita etiam eorum laborem et impugnationem nunquam fueras expertus.
chunks.english: Just as you are far from the constancy of purity of all these, so also you have never experienced their labor and assault.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quam ob rem quia perfectorum et in castitate perseverantium omnimodo discrepas meritis atque labore, videamus si aut eorum, quorum impudicitiae et irreligiositati minus discrepare videris, tribulationi et impugnationi, quam pro poenitentia agenda pertulerunt, aliquatenus assimileris.
chunks.english: For which reason, since you differ in every way in merits and labor from the perfect and those persevering in chastity, let us see whether you are in any way similar to the tribulation and assault that those endured in doing penance—those whose impudicity and irreligiosity you seem to differ from less.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Legitur quippe in supradicta Vita Patrum quia fuerit quidem vir vitam turpissimam super omnes vicinos suos ducens, ita ut opinatissimus in flagitiis haberetur.
chunks.english: For it is read in the aforementioned Lives of the Fathers that there was a man leading a most shameful life beyond all his neighbors, so that he was held most notorious in wickedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hic aliquando Dei miseratione compunctus ad poenitentiam convertitur, et intra sepulcrum quoddam se concludens, priorum scelerum pollutiones lacrymarum fontibus diluebat.
chunks.english: This man, at some point moved to compunction by God's mercy, turned to penance, and shutting himself within a certain tomb, washed away the pollutions of his former crimes with fountains of tears.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque in his posito hebdomada transisset, nocte veniunt ad eum daemones in sepulcro clamantes et dicentes: «Quid est quod agis, o impurissime et flagitiosissime? Postquam omni spurcitia satiatus es, nunc a nobis castus et religiosus effugere cupis? Nolis, velis, amodo unus ex nobis eris.»
chunks.english: And when a week had passed with him thus situated, at night demons came to him in the tomb, crying out and saying: 'What are you doing, O most impure and wicked one? After being satiated with every filth, now you wish to flee from us as chaste and religious? Whether you will it or not, from now on you will be one of us.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec et hujusmodi plura exprobrantibus eis ille jacebat immobilis, ne auditum quidem suum convertens ad eos, nec ullo penitus sermone respondens.
chunks.english: As they reproached him with these and many similar things, he lay immovable, not even turning his hearing toward them, nor responding with any word at all.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque illi saepius eadem repeterent, nec ille omnino moveretur, tunc in furorem commoti daemones, verberibus eum affixerunt multisque cruciatum suppliciis semivivum reliquerunt.
chunks.english: And when they repeated the same things many times and he was not moved at all, then the demons, stirred into fury, struck him with blows and, torturing him with many torments, left him half-dead.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ille tamen tot cruciatibus nec moveri potuit a loco in quo orationis causa jacuerat.
chunks.english: Yet even with so many tortures, he could not be moved from the place where he had lain for the sake of prayer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sequenti vero nocte daemones iterum venientes gravioribus eum verberibus cruciabant; sed nec tunc quidem moveri valuit a loco, melius esse dicens mortem subire quam ultro daemonibus obedire.
chunks.english: On the following night the demons came again, torturing him with more severe blows. But not even then could he be moved from the place, saying it was better to undergo death than to obey the demons of his own accord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nihilominus tertia nocte multitudo daemonum convenit, et absque ulla miseratione in eum irruentes, omnibus poenis cruciatibusque afficiunt.
chunks.english: Nevertheless, on the third night a multitude of demons assembled, and rushing upon him without any mercy, they afflicted him with every pain and torture.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque jam corpus defecisset in suppliciis, extremus tamen spiritus obsistebat imperio daemonum.
chunks.english: And when his body had already failed under the torments, nevertheless his last breath resisted the command of the demons.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod ubi viderunt, exclamantes voce magna: Vicisti, inquiunt, vicisti!
chunks.english: When they saw this, crying out with a loud voice, they said: 'You have conquered, you have conquered!'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid ergo tibi in hujusmodi impugnatione videtur? Nunquid quia te supra multos occultis et manifestis tribulationibus oppressum esse saepius aestimabas, tale aliquid unquam passus eras? Quinimo longe minoribus subjacebas tentationibus.
chunks.english: What, then, does this assault seem to you? Did you, because you often considered yourself oppressed above many by hidden and manifest tribulations, ever suffer anything like this? On the contrary, you were subject to far lesser temptations.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed licet exemplo satis hoc sis victus ab uno, pro eo tamen quod in occultis et spiritalibus tentationibus maxime te vexatum arbitraris, aliud in quo spiritalis tantum impugnatio declaratur, proferre libet exemplum.
