chunks.english: But although you are sufficiently overcome by this one example, yet because you judge yourself to be especially tormented in hidden and spiritual temptations, I wish to present another example in which only spiritual assault is shown.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Legitur namque de Maria Aegyptiaca, quoniam inter caetera quae Zozimo abbati de vitae conversionisque suae qualitate referebat, dixit: «Crede mihi, abba, septemdecim annis feris et irrationabilibus luctabar desideriis.»
chunks.english: For it is read of Mary of Egypt that among other things she told Abbot Zosimas about the character of her life and conversion, she said: 'Believe me, abba, for seventeen years I struggled with wild and irrational desires.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et post pauca: «Cogitationes autem quae ad fornicationem iterum compellebant me quomodo tibi enarrare possum? Ignis interim infelix corpus meum nimius succendebat et me per omnia exurebat, et ad desiderium commistionis trahebat.
chunks.english: And after a little: 'But how can I describe to you the thoughts that compelled me again to fornication? An unhappy fire meanwhile burned my body excessively and consumed me completely, and dragged me to the desire of intercourse.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dum ergo talis accenderet cogitatio, prosternebam me in terram, et lacrymis eam infundens ipsam, mihi veraciter adstare sperabam quae me fide jusserat, id est sanctissimam Dei Genitricem: et sic per septemdecim annos periculis multis, ut dixi, luctabar.»
chunks.english: Therefore, when such a thought inflamed me, I would prostrate myself on the ground and, flooding it with tears, I hoped that she who had commanded me in faith—that is, the most holy Mother of God—truly stood by me. And thus for seventeen years I struggled with many dangers, as I said.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce vides quantum non solum virilis, sed etiam femineus sexus laboraverit et pro integritatis constantia, et pro corruptionis poenitentia.
chunks.english: Behold, you see how much not only the male sex but also the female sex labored both for the constancy of integrity and for penance for corruption.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nunquid ergo septemdecim annis jam laborasti pugnans contra cogitationes desideriorum carnalium? Quid autem tot annos dico, qui nec uno passus es tanta anno integro?
chunks.english: Have you then already labored for seventeen years fighting against thoughts of carnal desires? But why do I speak of so many years, when you have not endured such things for even one full year?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nondum igitur laboris viam coepisti, et tamen velut ex labore nimio jam fatigatus defecisti.
chunks.english: Therefore you have not yet begun the path of labor, and yet, as if from excessive labor, you have already given up exhausted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et si quas tentationis et adversitatis molestias perferres, nunquid propterea patientiae et spei solatia deponere debes?
chunks.english: But even if you were bearing whatever troubles of temptation and adversity, should you therefore lay down the consolations of patience and hope?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonne et ex supra commemoratis sanctorum Patrum exemplis, et ex aliis Scripturae sacrae sententiis agnoscere potes, utile esse ac necessarium perferre tentationes?
chunks.english: Can you not recognize from the above-mentioned examples of the holy Fathers and from other sayings of sacred Scripture that it is useful and necessary to endure temptations?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Petrus namque apostolus dicit: Charissimi, nolite peregrinari in fervore, qui ad tentationem vobis sit, quasi aliquid novi vobis contingat; sed communicantes Christi passionibus gaudete.
chunks.english: For Peter the apostle says: 'Beloved, do not be surprised at the fiery trial when it comes upon you to test you, as though something strange were happening to you. But rejoice insofar as you share Christ's sufferings.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jacobus etiam apostolus docet dicens: Omne gaudium existimate, fratres mei, cum in tentationes varias incideritis, scientes quod probatio fidei vestrae patientiam operatur, patientia autem opus perfectum habet, ut sitis perfecti et integri, in nullo deficientes.
chunks.english: James the apostle also teaches, saying: 'Count it all joy, my brothers, when you fall into various trials, knowing that the testing of your faith produces patience. And let patience have its perfect work, that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Item apostolus idem dicit: Beatus vir qui suffert tentationem, quoniam, cum probatus fuerit, accipiet coronam vitae, quam repromisit Deus diligentibus se.
chunks.english: Likewise the same apostle says: 'Blessed is the man who endures trial, for when he has been approved, he will receive the crown of life that God has promised to those who love him.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc et Paulus apostolus ait: Gloriamur in tribulationibus, scientes quod tribulatio patientiam operatur, patientia autem probationem, probatio vero spem, spes autem non confundit.
chunks.english: Hence also Paul the apostle says: 'We boast in our tribulations, knowing that tribulation produces patience, and patience produces proven character, and proven character produces hope, and hope does not disappoint.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hujusmodi ergo documenta et in Novo et in Veteri Testamento inveniuntur multa: quibus si jugiter intendis, non solum tentationes quaslibet advenientes facilius devincis, sed et majoris intelligentiae dona consequeris.
chunks.english: Many such teachings are found both in the New and in the Old Testament. If you constantly attend to them, you not only more easily overcome whatever temptations come, but you also obtain the gifts of greater understanding.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vir enim, ut scriptum est, in multis expertus cogitabit multa; et qui multa didicit, narrabit intellectum.
chunks.english: For as it is written: 'A man who has been tested in many things will think many thoughts; and he who has learned much will display understanding.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cujus videlicet rei indicium in temetipso probare vales, quia, nisi varia tentationum molestia et eura exercitatus esses, nequaquam tanta Scripturarum documenta aut testimonia tractare studuisses. Plura quippe prius incognita jam indagationi tuae sunt reserata.
chunks.english: You can prove an indication of this matter in yourself, because unless you had been exercised by the varied trouble and care of temptations, you would never have studied to treat so many teachings or testimonies of Scripture. For many things previously unknown have now been opened to your investigation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illud quoque summopere notandum tibi est quia, si absque tentatione et tribulatione aliqua diutius remaneres, gravius periculosiusque de beneficiorum meorum largitate superbiendo caderes, quam cum sceleribus omnimodis quondam deditus esses.
chunks.english: This also must especially be noted by you: that if you were to remain longer without any temptation and tribulation, you would fall more gravely and dangerously through pride in the generosity of my benefits than when you were once given over to crimes of every kind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quanto enim excellentioribus quisque dotatur donis, tanto magis pulsatur vitio elationis.
chunks.english: For the more excellent the gifts with which someone is endowed, the more he is struck by the vice of pride.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et ille praestantissimus angelorum non sustinens dignitatis suae donum, eo gravius superbiendo cecidit quo sublimior caeteris exstitit.
chunks.english: Hence that most outstanding of angels, not sustaining the gift of his dignity, fell through pride all the more gravely because he was more sublime than the others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saul quoque, ut liber Regum narrat, cum ante perceptam regni dignitatem humilis mihique esset acceptus, postea vero ex eadem dignitate superbiens a me est repulsus.
chunks.english: Saul also, as the book of Kings narrates, when before receiving the dignity of kingship he was humble and acceptable to me, afterward, becoming proud from that same dignity, was rejected by me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipse etiam David, licet regum eximius et pietati meae placitur esset, prius tamen quam ad regni elevaretur fastigia majori se constrinxit religionis cura.
chunks.english: David himself also, although he was outstanding among kings and pleasing to my mercy, nevertheless before he was elevated to the heights of kingship, bound himself with greater care of religion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam antea bona pro malis, postea vero mala reddidit pro bonis: ideoque iniquitatis suae non immemor, in centesimo primo psalmo gemebundus ad me ait: Quia elevans allisisti me;
chunks.english: For earlier he returned good for evil, but later he returned evil for good. And therefore, not unmindful of his iniquity, groaning in the one hundred and second psalm, he said to me: 'Because lifting me up, you have cast me down.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ac si aperte diceret: Quanto sublimiore me dignitate elevasti, tanto majoribus peccatis me implicari permisisti.
chunks.english: As if he were saying openly: 'The more you elevated me with sublime dignity, the more you permitted me to be entangled in greater sins.'
chunks.notes: Referring to David's adultery with Bathsheba

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea quantae ruinae is, qui prae caeteris elevatur, pateat, liber Paralipomenon indicat. De Joas namque rege Juda refert, quia post mortem Joiadae sacerdotis, qui eum ad regnum provexit, in tantam elationem venerit ut, relicto cultu Dei, a suis se permiserit adorari.
chunks.english: Moreover, how great a ruin is open to him who is elevated above others is indicated by the book of Chronicles. For it reports of Joash, king of Judah, that after the death of the priest Jehoiada, who promoted him to the kingdom, he came to such pride that, abandoning the worship of God, he allowed himself to be adored by his people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pro quo superbiae flagitio quod regi eidem evenerit Scriptura subjungit, dicens: Cumque volutus esset onnus, ascendit contra eum rex Syriae, venitque in Judam et Jerusalem, et interfecit cunctos principes populi, et universam praedam miserunt regi Damascum. In Joas quoque ignominiosa exercuerunt judicia, et abeuntes dimiserunt eum in languoribus magnis.
chunks.english: For this crime of pride, what befell that same king, Scripture adds, saying: 'And when the year had turned, the king of Syria came up against him and came into Judah and Jerusalem, and killed all the princes of the people, and sent all the spoil to the king of Damascus. They also executed disgraceful judgments upon Joash, and departing left him in great sicknesses.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex quibus omnibus sentiri valet, quia nemo aut virtutis aut dignitatis alicujus excellentia sublimatus, mortiferae superbiae ruinam absque magna contritione evadet.
chunks.english: From all these things it can be understood that no one elevated by excellence of any virtue or dignity will escape the ruin of deadly pride without great contrition.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et beatus Paulus hoc attestatur, qui patefactis quibusdam virtutibus sibi concessis subjungit, dicens: Et ne magnitudo revelationum extollat me, datus est mihi stimulus carnis meac angelus Satanae ut me colaphizet.
chunks.english: But blessed Paul also attests to this, who, having disclosed certain virtues granted to him, adds, saying: 'And lest the greatness of the revelations should exalt me, there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, an angel of Satan to buffet me.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim tantus vir stimulis ideo premebatur carnalibus, ne pro concessis extolleretur virtutibus, putasne tibi opus est aliqua adversitate fatigari, ne de perceptis beneficiis praesumas gloriari?
chunks.english: For if so great a man was pressed by carnal thorns for this reason—lest he be exalted because of the virtues granted to him—do you think you need to be wearied by some adversity, lest you presume to glory in the benefits you have received?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quam ob rem perpende, quaeso, tentationis tuae causam et ordinem, exordium et finem, ut agnoscere possis, cui postremo amariori quam primitus et illa, quam tu insolitam vocasti tentationem, vexatus fueris.
chunks.english: For which reason, consider, I ask you, the cause and order of your temptation, its beginning and end, so that you may recognize by what ultimately more bitter temptation you have been vexed than at first—even that temptation which you called unusual.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primis namque ideo levioribus solitisque permissus es fatigari tentationibus, ut quasi per gradus quosdam ad altiora paulatim ascenderes et disceres congredi quandoque cum gravioribus.
chunks.english: For in the first place you were permitted to be wearied by lighter and usual temptations, so that, as if by certain steps, you might gradually ascend to higher things and learn to contend at some point with more serious ones.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed cum in tam levi et temperata impugnatione aliquantulum proficiens, ea quae in te gratia mea operabatur, magis ac magis viribus meritisque tuis attribueres;
chunks.english: But when, making some progress in such light and moderate assault, you attributed more and more what my grace was working in you to your own strengths and merits,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: in talem te tandem permisi tentationem incurrere, cujus impugnationis magnitudine depressus, agnosceres cui instantem defectum, et cui praeteritum omnemque laboris tui profectum debuisses imputare:
chunks.english: I finally permitted you to fall into such a temptation that, pressed down by the magnitude of the assault, you might recognize to whom you should attribute your present failure, and to whom your past progress and all your labor's advancement:

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ac per hoc tanto certior de gratiae meae beneficiis existeres, quanto minus de meritis tuis praesumeres.
chunks.english: And through this you might be all the more certain about the benefits of my grace, the less you presumed about your own merits.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Author's Literary Works and Divine Inspiration
chunks.latin: PARS SECUNDA.
chunks.english: Part Two.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc ideo clericus supradictus ideo scripsit, ut his qui in conversionis initio sacram Scripturam legere cupiunt, ostenderet qualiter immensam diabolicae fraudis astutiam, qua omnes eamdem Scripturam legentes impugnare solet, agnoscere et praecavere, qualiterque etiam divinae inspirationis gratiam agnoscere et invocare debeant.
chunks.english: The aforementioned cleric wrote this so that he might show to those who at the beginning of their conversion desire to read sacred Scripture how they should recognize and guard against the immense cunning of diabolical fraud—by which he usually attacks all who read that Scripture—and also how they should recognize and invoke the grace of divine inspiration.
chunks.notes: Referring back to the dialogue/temptation in Part One

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scripsit et idem clericus multa alia, quaedam quidem dictando, quaedam autem alio modo, quae scilicet utraque subsequenter pandere volo; sed dictata prius, post haec quoque caetera pandam.
chunks.english: The same cleric also wrote many other things, some indeed by dictation, others in another manner—both of which I wish to reveal in what follows. But I will disclose the dictated works first, and afterward the others.
chunks.notes: The author now begins describing his own works in third person

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic ergo incipit scribere de librorum dictamine. Quia igitur alternis uti delectabile est, non solum ob depellendam taedii molestiam, sed etiam ob recreandas animae et corporis vires studui quondam dicta diversae qualitatis scribere, sed nunc reserare libet quae scripserim.
chunks.english: Thus he begins to write concerning the composition of books. Because it is delightful to make use of alternating things, not only to drive away the trouble of tedium but also to restore the powers of soul and body, I once endeavored to write sayings of diverse character, but now I wish to reveal what I have written.
chunks.notes: Shifting to first person as Otloh discusses his methodology

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tres namque libellos diverso tempore diversoque stylo edidi in primis, quos volui in unum volumen, quasi ad unam convescentium mensam colligere, instar videlicet trium pulmentorum, quae nobis sanctus Pater Benedictus coenantibus largiri jubet in regula, ut qui ex uno nequiverit refici, ex alio reficiatur.
chunks.english: For I produced three little books at different times and in different styles at first, which I wished to gather into one volume, as if at one table for those feasting—like the three dishes which holy Father Benedict commands to be provided for us dining in the Rule, so that whoever cannot be refreshed by one may be refreshed by another.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Confido itaque in Domino, quia omni spirituali cibo delectatus in aliquo illorum inveniat, per quod spiritualiter refici valeat.
chunks.english: Therefore I trust in the Lord that anyone delighted with spiritual food will find in some of them something by which he may be spiritually refreshed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed haec de omnibus generaliter sint dicta. Nunc vero reserare cupio quae causa fuerit singulos scribere, ut lector aliquatenus exinde instructus noverit me nequaquam frustra in talibus desudasse.
chunks.english: But let these things be said generally about all. Now, however, I desire to reveal what the reason was for writing each one, so that the reader, instructed to some extent from this, may know that I have by no means labored in vain in such things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primum ergo libellum metrice prolatum, cui imposui titulum: De Spiritali doctrina, scribere hujusmodi erat causa.
chunks.english: Therefore the reason for writing the first little book, set forth in verse, to which I gave the title 'On Spiritual Doctrine,' was of this kind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum enim quondam ex infirmitate maxima convalescens ad monasticae professionis vitam venissem, tunc in brevi, plusquam credibile sit, factus sum sospes: ex qua sospitate veritus ne aliquod damnum spirituale mihi oriretur, prece Dominum intima rogavi ne me in tanta sospitate positum otio inutili torpescere, sed aliqua tentatione, quam mihi congruere sciret, pulsari permitteret.
chunks.english: For when I once came to the life of monastic profession recovering from very great illness, then in a short time—more than is credible—I became well. Out of this wellness, fearing lest some spiritual harm should arise for me, I prayed to the Lord with intimate prayer that he would not allow me, placed in such wellness, to grow sluggish in useless idleness, but permit me to be tested by some temptation that he knew would be appropriate for me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post hanc orationem non multum temporis fluxit: et ecce variae tentationum molestiae accedentes in tantum me circumdederunt, ut et incaute me orasse et magis ad interitum quam ad proventum exauditum esse aliquando vererer.
chunks.english: After this prayer not much time passed, and behold, various troubles of temptations approaching surrounded me so much that I sometimes feared I had prayed incautiously and had been heard more for destruction than for benefit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed saepius retractans illud apostolicum: Fidelis Deus, qui non patietur vos tentari supra id quod potestis, sed faciet cum tentatione proventum ut possitis sustinere;
chunks.english: But frequently reconsidering that apostolic saying: 'God is faithful, who will not allow you to be tempted beyond what you are able, but will make with the temptation also a way out so that you may be able to endure'—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: laboravi non quantum debui, sed quantum fragilitate mea, permittente ipsoque Domino adjuvante, potui, ne in conspectu adversariorum meorum corruerem.
chunks.english: I labored not as much as I should have, but as much as I could with my frailty, with the Lord permitting and himself helping, lest I fall in the sight of my adversaries.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideoque in tanta molestia tentationis, quae eo magis imminebat, quo major sospitas corporalis inerat, omnimodo tractare coepi, quali studio, qualique labore corpus spiritui subjicerem.
chunks.english: And so in such great trouble of temptation, which pressed all the more because greater bodily health was present, I began to consider in every way by what zeal and by what labor I might subject the body to the spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam ea quae communiter cum caeteris fratribus in coenobio agere docebar, sed et illa quae speciali devotione scribendo aut legendo, seu etiam jejunando sponte subii, non satis affligere corpus videbantur.
chunks.english: For those things that I was taught to do commonly with the other brothers in the monastery, and also those things that I undertook of my own accord with special devotion by writing or reading, or even fasting, did not seem to afflict the body enough.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque diu tractarem quo potissimum studio memet in tantis periculis constitutum aptissime jugiterque constringerem, occurrit animo ut in dictamine me occuparem aliquo:
chunks.english: And when I considered for a long time by what zeal especially I might most aptly and constantly constrain myself placed in such great dangers, it occurred to my mind that I should occupy myself with some composition.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quod et saepe expertus sum mentem lascivam cujuslibet scholastice instructi in nullo posse magis constringi quam studio dictandi.
chunks.english: For I have often experienced that the lascivious mind of anyone scholastically trained can be constrained in nothing more than in the practice of composition.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hujusmodi igitur occasione accepta, libellum primum scribere coepi metrico scilicet stylo, quo maxime in saeculari vita positus me exercebam;
chunks.english: Having taken this kind of occasion, then, I began to write a first little book—namely in metrical style, in which I exercised myself especially when I was in secular life.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ponens in eo varias spiritualis doctrinae sententias, quibus me solummodo contra tentationes imminentes instruens roboravi;
chunks.english: Placing in it various sayings of spiritual doctrine, by which I instructed and strengthened myself alone against the temptations pressing upon me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: aut pravitatis meae quantitatem, quam cum caeteris clericis communiter in mundo exercebam, pertinaciamque quam specialiter prae multis ibidem retinebam, magis attenderem atque litteris expositam majori poenitentia diluendam agnoscerem.
chunks.english: Or so that I might pay more attention to the extent of my wickedness, which I practiced commonly with other clerics in the world, and to the obstinacy that I retained there especially beyond many—and, having set it forth in writing, might recognize it should be washed away with greater penance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inserui in eodem etiam libello sermonem quemdam lamentabili stylo editum, prius quidem disputans de diversa clericorum negligentia nec non avaritia: deinde narrans de miserabili improbitatis meae vindicta tam spiritualiter quam corporaliter patrata.
chunks.english: I also inserted in the same little book a certain discourse set forth in a lamentable style, first indeed disputing concerning the various negligence and also avarice of clerics, then narrating concerning the miserable punishment of my wickedness enacted both spiritually and bodily.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ignorabam enim tunc, si quo loco proferenda forent hujusmodi dicta, quae licet in modo sint posita, post caetera tamen, cum me aliquid perspicaciori intuitu agnoscere coepissem, a me constant edita;
chunks.english: For I was then ignorant in what place such sayings should be brought forth, which, though placed in verse, nevertheless were produced by me after other things, when I had begun to recognize something with more perceptive insight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quia metricis prolatus est verbis quae pueris et intrantibus ad discendas litteras a multis primitus exhiberi solent.
chunks.english: Because it is set forth in metrical words that are usually first presented by many to boys and beginners for learning letters.
chunks.notes: Referring to teaching verse to students

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primis quoque ponere decrevi ut ex hoc quilibet ad veritatis viam conversus sumat quasi prandium conversioni suae congruum, sicque leviori cibo refectus ad lautiores dapes sacrae doctrinae capiendas aptior accedat.
chunks.english: I also decided to place it first so that from this anyone converted to the way of truth might take, as it were, a meal fitting for his conversion, and thus, refreshed with lighter food, might approach more aptly to take up the richer fare of sacred doctrine.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Librum vero visionum, licet post duos scripserim, in ordine tamen secundum ideo posui, ut illa doctrina, quae in priori libello variis prolata est sententiis, in hoc quoque variis corroboraretur exemplis;
chunks.english: But the book of visions, although I wrote it after two others, I nevertheless placed second in order, so that that doctrine which was set forth in various sayings in the earlier booklet might also be strengthened in this one by various examples.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: eaque tenacius praemissa animo inhaereant verba, quae majori testimonio tam pietatis quam severitatis divinae in subsequentibus commendantur,
chunks.english: And so that the words sent ahead might cling more tenaciously to the mind, which are commended in what follows by greater testimony both of mercy and of divine severity—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: in morem videlicet lautioris cibi, qui primo aqua pura coctus, deinde aliqua aceti vel etiam piperis adjectione accuratius praeparatur.
chunks.english: In the manner, that is, of richer food, which is first cooked in pure water, then more carefully prepared by the addition of some vinegar or even pepper.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sola enim lenitatis verba in docendo prolata, quasi quidam cibi in sola aqua excocti possunt intelligi;
chunks.english: For words of gentleness alone brought forth in teaching can be understood as certain foods cooked only in water.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: sed cum dehinc aliqua severitatis exempla admiscentur, velut amaro aceti seu piperis additamento condita fercula ut suaviora efficiantur, non incongrue intelliguntur.
chunks.english: But when thereafter some examples of severity are mixed in, they are not inappropriately understood as dishes seasoned with the bitter addition of vinegar or pepper to make them more pleasing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Apparet igitur quia, sicut cibus tam amaris quam suavibus condimentis coctus avidius sumitur, ita et doctrina blandimentis quidem inchoata, sed deinde alicujus severitatis commista exemplis studiosius auditur.
chunks.english: It is clear, therefore, that just as food cooked with both bitter and sweet seasonings is taken more eagerly, so also doctrine begun with enticements but then mixed with examples of some severity is heard more eagerly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et Apostolus doctorem quemlibet instruens ait: Argue, obsecra, increpa, id est blandimenta terroribus admisce.
chunks.english: Hence also the Apostle, instructing any teacher, says: 'Argue, entreat, rebuke'—that is, mix enticements with terrors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: His igitur praelibatis de secundi ordine libelli, jam vero subjungere volo ubi et qua occasione scriptum fuerit.
chunks.english: Having thus prefaced these things about the second book in order, now I wish to add where and on what occasion it was written.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ergo monasterium nostrum in urbe Ratisbona constitutum varia episcoporum persecutione destrui viderem, ibique per triginta annos meliora sperans subsisterem, occulto Dei judicio longe aliter quam sperarem evenit.
chunks.english: When, therefore, I saw our monastery established in the city of Regensburg being destroyed by various persecutions of bishops, and remained there for thirty years hoping for better things, by the hidden judgment of God it turned out far differently than I hoped.
chunks.notes: Autobiographical detail about St Emmeram's monastery in Regensburg

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qualiter autem destructio tanta evenerit, quia nec breviter proferri valet, nec ineptias tantas hic exponere libet, aliis proferenda relinquo:
chunks.english: How such great destruction occurred, because it cannot be briefly stated, nor do I wish to set forth such great follies here, I leave to be brought forth by others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: hoc solummodo enarrans, quia cum exteriora et interiora monasterii nostri commoda penitus destrui sensissem, et contra haec nil praevaluissem, quin imo a fratribus quibusdam juvenibus quibus displicebam apud episcopum accusatus, varias mihi minas ab illo illiusque familiaribus agi saepius audissem;
chunks.english: Narrating only this: that when I perceived the external and internal goods of our monastery to be completely destroyed, and I could do nothing against these things—indeed, accused before the bishop by certain young brothers to whom I was displeasing, I often heard that various threats were being made against me by him and his associates—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: tunc, petita ab abbate licentia, ad monasterium Fuldense quasi cito reversurus perrexi.
chunks.english: Then, having sought permission from the abbot, I proceeded to the monastery of Fulda as if about to return soon.
chunks.notes: Fulda was one of the great monasteries of medieval Germany

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verumtamen non dubito quin tantam destructionem nemo in nos agere posset, nisi justo Dei judicio peccatisque nostris exigentibus permissus:
chunks.english: Nevertheless, I do not doubt that no one could have brought such destruction upon us unless permitted by the just judgment of God and our sins demanding it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quod scilicet exinde palam datur intelligi, quia in ipso discessionis meae anno, qui erat ab incarnatione Domini 1062, miserando semperque lugendo igne combustum est coenobium nostrum.
chunks.english: Which is clearly given to be understood from this: that in the very year of my departure, which was the year ten sixty-two from the incarnation of the Lord, our monastery was consumed by a fire to be pitied and always mourned.
chunks.notes: Historical date: 1062 CE, fire at St Emmeram's Regensburg

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Fuldensi igitur monasterio positus, et optatae tranquillitatis atque charitatis dono ibidem potitus, frequenter tractavi, qualiter pro ejusdem tranquillitatis gratia Deo, a quo mihi data est, grates referrem.
chunks.english: Therefore, placed in the monastery of Fulda and having obtained there the gift of desired tranquility and charity, I frequently considered how I might give thanks to God—from whom it was given to me—for the grace of that same tranquility.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Magnum quippe mihi tunc videbatur in eodem monasterio pacifico incessu posse deambulare.
chunks.english: For it seemed great to me then to be able to walk with peaceful steps in that same monastery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quapropter animum ad deferendas Deo gratias omnino convertens, et si qua dictandi materia apte occurreret diutius exquirens, reperi tandem quamdam dictandi occasionem, visiones videlicet, quas et ego quondam vidi et ab aliis per loca diversa profectus audivi.
chunks.english: Therefore, turning my mind entirely to giving thanks to God, and seeking for a long time if any suitable material for composition might occur, I finally found a certain occasion for composition—namely visions, which I once saw myself and heard from others as I traveled through various places.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Has namque ad multorum notitiam deferre ideo utile esse arbitrabar, quia credo ad omnes homines pertinere quoties quilibet visitatur a Deo, aut per castigationem aut per consolationem, sicut et ipse in Evangelio dicit: Quod uni dico, omnibus dico.
chunks.english: For I judged it useful to bring these to the knowledge of many, because I believe it pertains to all people whenever anyone is visited by God, either through chastisement or through consolation, as he himself says in the Gospel: 'What I say to one, I say to all.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Incitabar etiam ex hoc ad scribendum, quia, cum olim libellos duos ediderim, tertium pro sanctae Trinitatis honore, si ipsa Trinitas dignaretur inspirare, saepius optavi addere.
chunks.english: I was also urged to write from this: that since I had formerly published two little books, I often wished to add a third for the honor of the Holy Trinity, if that Trinity itself would deign to inspire me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tali itaque causa incitatus, librum visionum, de quo jam locutus sum, scribere studui.
chunks.english: Urged therefore by such a reason, I strove to write the book of visions of which I have already spoken.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tertium vero libellum, quem ante annos 15, ut reor, scripsi, cur in extremo ordine posuerim primo dicendum, deinde qua causa scriptus fuerit subnectendum.
chunks.english: But the third little book, which I wrote about fifteen years ago, I believe—why I placed it in the last order must first be explained, then on what occasion it was written must be added.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia enim multorum accuratis epulis coenantium mos est, ut in prima et secunda vice viliora quaeque fercula (quae non ideo viliora dicimus, quasi sint vilia, sed quod sint optimis inferiora); in extrema vero lautiora, et aliqua arte praecipua cocta sibi apponere jubeant,
chunks.english: Because the custom of many dining with careful meals is that in the first and second course they order somewhat inferior dishes (which we do not call inferior as if they were worthless, but because they are less than the best), but in the last course richer dishes, prepared with some special art.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: cupientes in hoc non tam suae gulae, quam aliorum, qui forte invitati sunt ad convivium, delectationi et honori deservire:
chunks.english: Desiring in this to serve not so much their own appetite as the delight and honor of others who happen to be invited to the banquet.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ego econtra simile aliquid inoliter volui in extremo aliqua lautioris sententiae verbula ante faciem legentium, quasi ad mensam convescentium ponere, quae quilibet lector avidus, licet indigno stylo edita, generosae tamen materiae dignitatem retinentia, pro dapibus lautis potuisset gustare.
chunks.english: I, on the contrary, wished unusually to place last some words of richer teaching before the face of readers, as if at a table of feasters, which any eager reader, though set forth in an unworthy style yet retaining the dignity of noble subject matter, could taste as rich fare.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam quae sententia lautior et suavior valet esse quam de gratia Dei, et cur credenda dicendaque sit gratia; judicia quoque quam vera justaque et necessaria sint;
chunks.english: For what teaching can be richer and sweeter than concerning the grace of God, and why grace must be believed and spoken of; also how true, just, and necessary are God's judgments;
chunks.notes: Describing the content of his third book

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: sed et quantam bene agendi facultatem a Deo concessam habeamus nisibus totis inquirere atque pluribus testimoniis approbare?
chunks.english: And also to inquire with all efforts and to prove with many testimonies how great a capacity for acting well we have been granted by God?
chunks.notes: Themes: grace, divine judgment, human free will

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec quippe tria per trium quaestionum solutiones in illo libro, quem in tertio locavi ordine, stylo quo potui per dialogum protuli, numero scilicet et ordine tali desiderans sanctam Trinitatem venerari.
chunks.english: These three things I set forth through the solutions of three questions in that book, which I placed in third order, in the style I could manage through dialogue, desiring by this number and order to venerate the Holy Trinity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et in hoc eamdem Trinitatem venerari studebam, quia in tertia quaestione, quam velut fercula quaedam praecipua ritu sagaci contexta, et allata in extremo ad lectionis coenam considentibus apposui;
chunks.english: But I also strove to venerate the same Trinity in this: that in the third question—which, like certain choice dishes woven together with wise custom and brought last to those sitting at the supper of reading, I set forth—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: de ipsa sancta Trinitate et Unitate, juxta intelligentiae meae vires, brevi et aperta probataque (ut spero) variis exemplis et argumentis ratione disserui.
chunks.english: I discussed the Holy Trinity and Unity itself according to the strength of my understanding, briefly and openly and (I hope) provedly by various examples and arguments of reason.
chunks.notes: Treatise on the Trinity in Book 3

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Spero etiam me in eodem libello omnibus tam in saeculari quam spirituali vita positis aliqua aedificationis verba protulisse, per quae quilibet esurientes et sitientes justitiam refici possunt.
chunks.english: I also hope that in the same little book I have brought forth for all—whether placed in secular or spiritual life—some words of edification, through which any who hunger and thirst for righteousness can be refreshed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec quidem de libri extremi ordine sint dicta, cui titulum imposui: De tribus quaestionibus, id est de divinae pietatis agnitione judiciorumque divinorum diversitate, nec non de varia bene agendi facultate.
chunks.english: Let these things be said concerning the last book in order, to which I gave the title: 'On Three Questions'—that is, on the recognition of divine mercy and the diversity of divine judgments, as well as on the varied capacity for acting well.
chunks.notes: Full title of the Dialogue/third book

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deinde vero subjungimus qua causa fuerit scriptus.
chunks.english: Then we add on what occasion it was written.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quidam namque monachus de Augiensi coenobio, nobilissima Alemannorum stirpe ortus, nomine Henricus, cum ab Jerusalem reverteretur, apud nos aliquandiu hospitatus est.
chunks.english: For a certain monk from the monastery of Reichenau, born of the most noble stock of the Alemanni, named Henry, when he was returning from Jerusalem, was our guest for some time.
chunks.notes: Reichenau (Augia) was a famous island monastery; Henry was returning from pilgrimage

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hic ergo quia in sacra Scriptura satis studiosus fuit, me frequenter quasi doctorem adiit, suppliciter rogans pro quibusdam sacrae Scripturae sententiis ut eas sibi exponerem.
chunks.english: Therefore, because he was quite studious in sacred Scripture, he frequently approached me as a teacher, humbly asking that I explain to him certain sayings of sacred Scripture.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ego autem humilitatem atque importunitatem petitionis ejus attendens, prout potui, ad quaeque rogata respondi.
chunks.english: But I, noticing the humility and persistence of his request, as I was able, responded to whatever he asked.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque haec collatio saepius inter nos habita sibi placeret, postremo illud deposcere coepit, ut eamdem collationem litteris exciperem.
chunks.english: And when this conversation held between us often pleased him, finally he began to request that I write down the same conversation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod licet diu tam pro desidia quam pro ignorantia differrem, aggressus sum tandem, scribens quidem per dialogum, sed sine titulo auctoris et absque personarum notarum litteris, ne facile pateret cujus epus esset.
chunks.english: Though I delayed this for a long time both out of laziness and ignorance, I finally undertook it, writing indeed through dialogue, but without the author's name and without identifying letters for the characters, lest it easily appear whose work it was.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inter haec vero ille ad monasterium suum vocatus proficiscitur, et post non multum tempus ad nos iterum revertitur.
chunks.english: Meanwhile he, summoned to his monastery, departed, and after not much time returned to us again.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc videns quae scribere coepi, qualiterque ea adumbravi, unice petiit ut et causam scribendi illustrarem prologo, et utriusque personae, mei videlicet ac sui, memoriam patefacerem in dialogo:
chunks.english: Then, seeing what I had begun to write and how I had sketched it out, he especially asked that I both illuminate the reason for writing with a prologue and reveal the memory of both persons—namely myself and himself—in the dialogue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quod mox, ut poteram, scribens implere studebam.
chunks.english: Which I soon strove to fulfill by writing, as I was able.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec igitur causa Dialogum me scribere fecit: quam idcirco reseravi, ne videar frustra scriptis alia addere scripta; utque cognita charitate qua impellebar ad scribendum, quilibet in quorum manus iste liber venerit, eadem charitate incitentur ad legendum.
chunks.english: This, therefore, was the reason that made me write the Dialogue. I have revealed it so that I may not seem to add writings to writings in vain, and so that, the charity by which I was impelled to write being known, anyone into whose hands this book comes may be urged by the same charity to read.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec sint dicta de supradictis libris, quos in unum componere volui. Nunc etiam liber pandere qua causa studuerim alios libellos scribere.
chunks.english: Let these things be said about the above-mentioned books, which I wished to compose into one. Now I also freely disclose on what occasion I endeavored to write other little books.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum igitur persecutionem quam in monasterio nostro (ut supradictum est) passus eram, fugiens ad Fuldense monasterium venirem, ibique per annos quatuor cum tranquillitate magna commoratus viderer;
chunks.english: When therefore, fleeing the persecution that I had suffered in our monastery (as mentioned above), I came to the monastery of Fulda, and remained there for four years with great tranquility,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: dictandi scribendique studium frequentare postulabar a quibusdam ejusdem monasterii fratribus, ut sancti Bonifacii Vitam difficili stylo editam, aliquid facilius ederem.
chunks.english: I was asked by certain brothers of that monastery to pursue the practice of composing and writing, so that I might produce something easier than the Life of Saint Boniface, which was set forth in difficult style.
chunks.notes: Life of St Boniface—Fulda's patron saint, buried there

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quam petitionem, licet diu denegarem, postremo tamen compulsus importunis precibus, ut potui, quod petitus eram implevi.
chunks.english: This request, although I refused for a long time, nevertheless compelled by persistent prayers, I finally fulfilled as I was able.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Causa ergo tali librum sancti Bonifacii promptus scripsi: alium quoque libellum quem appellavi Manualem, pro admonitione clericorum et laicorum scriptum ibidem positus edidi.
chunks.english: For such a reason, then, I readily wrote the book of Saint Boniface. I also published another little book which I called the 'Manual,' written for the admonition of clerics and laypeople while I was situated there.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Item libellum Proverbiorum in eodem monasterio positus scribere coepi.
chunks.english: Likewise, I began to write a little book of Proverbs while situated in that same monastery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum autem assiduis epistolis per monasterii nostri fratres quosdam revocatus ad patriam nollem cito reverti propter illorum fratrum, inter quos tunc commorabar, petitionem (omnimodis enim precibus ut illos non desererem efflagitabant), vix tandem ab illis remeandi licentiam obtinui.
chunks.english: But when I was recalled to my homeland by constant letters through certain brothers of our monastery, I did not wish to return quickly because of the request of those brothers among whom I was then living (for they begged with every kind of prayer that I not abandon them)—I finally obtained permission to return from them with difficulty.
chunks.notes: Torn between Regensburg and Fulda communities

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Verumtamen ad monasterium nostrum prius pervenire nolui, quam indiciis aliquibus explorarem qualiter omnia agerentur ibidem. Qua de re ad monasterium Amerbach dictum perrexi, cupiens illic exspectare quousque certus fierem de monasterii nostri qualitate.
chunks.english: Nevertheless, I did not wish to arrive at our monastery first until I explored by certain signs how all things were being conducted there. For this reason I proceeded to the monastery called Amorbach, wishing to wait there until I became certain about the condition of our monastery.
chunks.notes: Amorbach monastery as waystation to assess situation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque ab ejusdem monasterii abbate susceptus omni humanitate et pietate retinerer, et de sacrae Scripturae quaestionibus multis inter nos sermo assiduus ageretur, placuissetque ei saepissime responsio mea,
chunks.english: And when I was received by the abbot of that monastery with all humanity and kindness, and constant conversation about many questions of sacred Scripture was conducted between us, and my response had very often pleased him,

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: accidit ut juxta paschalia festa dixisset ad me: Credite mihi quod si vobis praecipere aliquid possem, absque dubio praeciperem ut in hac solemnitate proxima sermonem faceretis ad populum.
chunks.english: It happened that near the Easter feast he said to me: 'Believe me that if I could command you anything, without doubt I would command that you give a sermon to the people at this approaching solemnity.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cui ego respondi dicens: Cur talia dicitis ad me, qui nihil hujus rei scio, qui nunquam consuevi populum alloqui in publico?
chunks.english: To whom I responded, saying: 'Why do you say such things to me, who know nothing of this matter, who have never been accustomed to address the people in public?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Illo vero eadem repetente, coepi mox cogitare intra me dicens: Quid facerem, si aliquis, cujus jussa transgredi non praesumerem, talia mihi praeciperet?
chunks.english: But when he repeated the same thing, I soon began to think within myself, saying: 'What would I do if someone whose commands I would not presume to transgress commanded such things of me?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde hujusmodi opus assumendum est mihi sponte, priusquam ab aliquo compellar potente, ut sic probem quid facturus essem.
chunks.english: Therefore such a work must be undertaken by me voluntarily, before I am compelled by someone powerful, so that I may thus prove what I would do.
chunks.notes: Better to volunteer than be forced

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Continuoque stylo accepto scribere coepi in modum sermonis, sumens exordium de Psalmistae dictis: Dominus de coelo prospicit super filios hominum: quae nimirum dicta, quantum potui, similitudinum argumentis roboravi, credens per haec aliquos aedificari;
chunks.english: And immediately, taking up my pen, I began to write in the manner of a sermon, taking the beginning from the saying of the Psalmist: 'The Lord looks down from heaven upon the children of men.' These words I strengthened as much as I could with arguments from similitudes, believing that through these some would be edified.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: cui videlicet operi titulum imposui: Quomodo legendum sit in rebus visibilibus.
chunks.english: To which work I gave the title: 'How One Should Read in Visible Things.'
chunks.notes: Sermon on reading nature as book of God

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum autem in eodem loco unum pene annum manerem, venerunt nuntii ex monasterio nostro missi, cum quibus exinde ad patriam reversus sum.
chunks.english: But when I remained in that same place for almost a year, messengers sent from our monastery came, with whom I then returned to my homeland.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: His igitur dictis, dicam quoque qua de causa caeteros scripserim libellos.
chunks.english: These things having been said, I will also tell on what occasion I wrote the other little books.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex petitione namque fratrum nostrorum, Vitam sancti Nicolai nec non sancti Wolfgangi emendans, sicut in utriusque vitae prologo intimatur, scripsi antequam proficiscerer ad monasterium Fuldense.
chunks.english: For at the request of our brothers, I wrote—emending the Life of Saint Nicholas as well as of Saint Wolfgang, as is indicated in the prologue of each life—before I set out for the monastery of Fulda.
chunks.notes: Lives of Nicholas and Wolfgang written before 1062

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et Vitam sancti Altonis, una cum quibusdam carminibus ad eumdem sanctum pertinentibus.
chunks.english: But also the Life of Saint Alto, together with certain verses pertaining to the same saint.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postquam vero redii, Vitam sancti Magni scripsi, compulsus fratrum duorum precibus intimis et assiduis, Wilhelmi scilicet ex congregatione nostra, et alterius qui ad nos discendi causa ex monasterio sancti Magni venit, Adalham dictus, quique nunc in Sanctae Afrae coenobio abbas est constitutus.
chunks.english: But after I returned, I wrote the Life of Saint Magnus, compelled by the intimate and persistent prayers of two brothers—namely Wilhelm from our congregation, and another who came to us from the monastery of Saint Magnus for the sake of learning, called Adalham, who is now established as abbot in the monastery of Saint Afra.
chunks.notes: Magnus of Füssen; Adalham became abbot at St Afra in Augsburg

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post haec autem cum viderem simul et audirem undique Christianae religionis destructionem, rectorum et principum negligentiam in subditos, tam in spirituali quam saeculari vita positos, doleremque jugiter pro talibus,
chunks.english: But after these things, when I both saw and heard everywhere the destruction of Christian religion, the negligence of rulers and princes toward their subjects—whether placed in spiritual or secular life—and constantly grieved for such things,
chunks.notes: Critique of church and society in mid-11th century

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: cogitare coepi, ut quia nullus dignaretur me audire communi sermone loquentem, pro miseriaque tanta condolentem, vel scribendo aliqua sacrae Scripturae verba proferrem, unde aliquos aedificare possem.
chunks.english: I began to think that, since no one deigned to hear me speaking in common discourse and grieving for such great misery, I might at least by writing bring forth some words of sacred Scripture by which I could edify some.
chunks.notes: Turning to writing when oral teaching rejected

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hac igitur causa scripsi librum mei quoque operis novissimum, cui titulum imposui: De cursu spirituali.
chunks.english: For this reason, therefore, I wrote the last book also of my work, to which I gave the title: 'On the Spiritual Course.'
chunks.notes: Liber de cursu spirituali—on the spiritual journey

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In quo videlicet libro quantum per sacrae Scripturae, maximeque per Psalterii et Evangelii campum currere potui, scribendo protuli.
chunks.english: In which book I set forth by writing as much as I could run through the field of sacred Scripture, especially through the Psalter and the Gospel.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inter haec et sermones quosdam nec non epistolas pro communi utilitate scripsi: quas si quis forte legere voluerit, apud nos invenire poterit.
chunks.english: Among these things I also wrote certain sermons as well as letters for the common good, which if anyone should wish to read, he will be able to find with us.
chunks.notes: Sermons and letters available but not included in this compilation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scripsi etiam ante plures annos librum De confessione actuum meorum, ut si qua infirmitas vel subitanea mors me in extremis impediret a debita confessione, saltem per scripta patefacerem quis ex memetipso, quis ex Dei gratia essem.
chunks.english: I also wrote several years ago a book 'On the Confession of My Deeds,' so that if any illness or sudden death should prevent me in my last moments from due confession, I might at least through writings reveal who I was of myself and who by God's grace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adhuc proferre libet duarum orationum verba. Attendens enim saepius, quia plurimos exemplis pravis seduxi, talia orationis verba scribere studui, in quibus unusquisque satis doceri potest quomodo pro se et pro aliis orare debeat:
chunks.english: I wish also to bring forth the words of two prayers. For often considering that I led many astray by wicked examples, I strove to write such words of prayer in which anyone can be sufficiently taught how he ought to pray for himself and for others.
chunks.notes: Two model prayers for personal and intercessory use

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: quae scilicet orationes cum a quibusdam jam habeantur, in hoc opusculo describere nolui.
chunks.english: Which prayers, since they are already held by certain persons, I did not wish to transcribe in this little work.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hae autem orationes aliaque superius de dictandi dono prolata, tanto magis pietati divinae constant referenda, quanto amplius notum est me vitiis plurimis quondam deditum nihil horum meruisse.
chunks.english: But these prayers and the other things brought forth above concerning the gift of composition must all the more be attributed to divine mercy, the more it is known that I, once devoted to very many vices, deserved none of these things.
chunks.notes: Attribution of all gifts to divine grace

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ideo autem haec protuli, ut juxta hoc quod ipse Dominus in Evangelio cuidam daemoniaco a se liberato praecepit dicens: Vade in domum tuam, et narra quanta fecerit tibi Deus, ego quoque narrarem quanta mihi beneficia Deus exhibuerit.
chunks.english: But I have brought forth these things so that, according to what the Lord himself commanded in the Gospel to a certain demoniac freed by him, saying: 'Go to your home and tell how much God has done for you,' I too might narrate how many benefits God has shown me.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde adhuc libet enarrare, quanta scientia quantaque facultas scribendi mihi data fuerit a Domino in primaeva aetate.
chunks.english: Hence I wish also to narrate how much knowledge and how great a capacity for writing was given to me by the Lord in my early age.
chunks.notes: Account of his youthful learning to follow

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum igitur parvus scholari disciplinae traditus fuissem litterasque celeriter didicissem, coepi etiam longe ante solitum tempus discendi, sine jussu magistri, artem discere scribendi.
chunks.english: When therefore I was handed over as a small boy to scholastic discipline and had learned letters quickly, I also began to learn the art of writing far before the usual time of learning, without the command of a teacher.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Furtivo enim et insolito modo, nec non sine docente nisus sum eamdem artem scribendi apprehendere.
chunks.english: For in a stealthy and unusual manner, and also without a teacher, I strove to grasp that same art of writing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qua de re contigit ut pennam ad scribendum inrecto usu retinere consuescerem, nec postea ab ullo docente super hoc corrigi valerem.
chunks.english: From which it happened that I became accustomed to hold the pen for writing in incorrect use, nor could I afterward be corrected on this by any teacher.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nimius namque usus prohibuit me emendare. Quod cum viderent plures, dixerunt omnes nunquam me bene scripturum.
chunks.english: For excessive habit prevented me from amending it. When many saw this, they all said I would never write well.
chunks.notes: Prediction of failure due to bad technique

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed aliter evenit ex gratia Dei, sicut plurimis notum est.
chunks.english: But it turned out otherwise by the grace of God, as is known to very many.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam cum in pueritia, ipsoque tempore quo tabula mihi data est cum aliis pueris ad discendam scripturam viderer aliquid scribere nosse, miraculum non parvum praebui videntibus:
chunks.english: For when in boyhood, at the very time when a writing tablet was given to me with other boys for learning writing, I seemed to know how to write something, I provided no small wonder to those watching.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: deinde vero non post longum tempus tam bene scribere coepi, tantumque affectum ad hoc habuit ut et in loco illo quo talia didici, id est in coenobio Tegernsee dicto, multos libros scripsissem;
chunks.english: Then indeed after not a long time I began to write so well, and had such great affection for this, that even in that place where I learned such things—that is, in the monastery called Tegernsee—I had written many books.
chunks.notes: Tegernsee monastery in Bavaria, early education site

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: et in Franciam translatus adhuc puer, ibique in tantum multa scribendo laborassem, ut inde rediens pene visu privatus fuissem.
chunks.english: And transferred to Francia while still a boy, and there I labored so much in writing many things that, returning from there, I had almost been deprived of sight.
chunks.notes: Went to France as youth; excessive copying damaged eyesight

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Haec ergo ideo proferre decrevi ut aliquos incitarem ad similem affectum laborandi; gratiamque Dei, quae tanta mihi beneficia praebuit, aliis narrans eos ad magnificandam eamdem gratiam Dei mecum traham.
chunks.english: Therefore I decided to bring forth these things so that I might incite some to similar affection for laboring, and by narrating to others the grace of God, which provided such great benefits to me, I might draw them to magnify with me that same grace of God.
chunks.notes: Purpose: encourage others to similar dedication

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et, ut hoc amplius faciam, proferre libet quantum laboraverim scribendo postea, cum reversus fuissem de Francia: ibi quippe fueram quando obiit Caesar Henricus, et factus est rex Conradus.
chunks.english: And to do this more fully, I wish to bring forth how much I labored in writing afterward, when I had returned from Francia—for I was there when Emperor Henry died and Conrad became king.
chunks.notes: Historical marker: death of Henry III (1056), succession of Conrad II as boy-king under regency

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postquam igitur in sancti Emmerammi coenobium ad conversionem veni, mox quorumdam prece pulsatus, tanto iterum scribendi studio occupabar, ut raro, nisi in festivis diebus aut in aliis horis incompetentibus, ab hoc opere cessarem.
chunks.english: After therefore I came to the monastery of Saint Emmeram for conversion, soon urged by the request of certain persons, I was occupied again with such great zeal for writing that rarely, except on feast days or at other unsuitable hours, did I cease from this work.
chunks.notes: Entered St Emmeram's as monk, prodigious copying output

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Interea successit et alius labor. Nam pro eo quod saepius legere, aut scribere, aut dictare videbar, scholasticorum cura mihi commissa est:
chunks.english: Meanwhile another labor also followed. For because I seemed often to read, or write, or compose, the care of students was committed to me.
chunks.notes: Made schoolmaster in addition to copying

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ex quibus nimirum omnibus ita per gratiam Dei constringebar, ut saepe corpus quieti necessariae non permitterer tradere.
chunks.english: From all of which indeed I was so bound by the grace of God that often I was not permitted to give my body to necessary rest.
chunks.notes: Overwork to point of sleep deprivation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque dictandi studium inesset, ad hoc saepissime non habui tempus, nisi in festivis diebus, aut noctibus, constrictus videlicet et puerorum cura ad docendum, et illorum petitione, quibus scribere coepi, ad scribendum.
chunks.english: And when the practice of composition was present, I most often did not have time for this except on feast days or at night, constrained both by the care of boys for teaching and by the request of those for whom I began to write, for writing.
chunks.notes: Composed original works only at night or feast days

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ergo praeter libellos a me dictatos, quos tam sponte quam petitus pro aliorum aedificatione dandos scripsi; uno minus XX libros Missales scripsi,
chunks.english: Therefore, besides the little books composed by me, which I wrote both voluntarily and when asked to be given for the edification of others, I wrote nineteen Missals—

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: decem quidem abbatibus aut fratribus in monasterio nostro positis; quatuor autem fratribus Fuldensibus, et quinque in aliis locis commorantibus;
chunks.english: Ten indeed for abbots or brothers placed in our monastery, four for brothers of Fulda, and five for those dwelling in other places.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: tres quoque libros Evangeliorum, et duos cum Epistolis et Evangeliis, qui Lectionarii vocantur. Ad haec etiam scripsi quatuor matutinales libros.
chunks.english: Also three Gospel books, and two with Epistles and Gospels, which are called Lectionaries. Besides these I also wrote four books for Matins.
chunks.notes: Total manuscript output: 19 Missals + 3 Gospels + 2 Lectionaries + 4 Matins books = 28 liturgical manuscripts

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea senectus et infirmitas varia me prohibuit, maximeque inquietudo diuturna, quae mihi ex variis curis et moerore pro monasterii nostri destructione exorto per tempora inolevit multa.
chunks.english: Afterward old age and various illness prevented me, and especially the long unrest that grew in me over many periods from various cares and grief arising from the destruction of our monastery.
chunks.notes: Work stopped due to age, illness, and distress over monastery troubles

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ei autem qui omne bonum praestat, et solus cuncta gubernat, quique mihi indigno concessit plurima dona, sit laus aeterna, sit honor per saecula cuncta.
chunks.english: But to him who bestows every good thing and alone governs all things, and who granted very many gifts to me, unworthy—to him be eternal praise, to him be honor through all ages.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libet etiam proferre, quantum reminisci valeo, quot libros quibusdam coenobiis aut amicis tradiderim.
chunks.english: I also wish to bring forth, as much as I can remember, how many books I handed over to certain monasteries or friends.
chunks.notes: Catalog of books given away begins

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et primo quidem fratres Fuldenses nominare volo, quia pro eo quod apud eos maxime laboravi, scribens libros multos, quos monasterio nostro tradebam; ideo apud nos quoque scripsi libros, quos ipsi non habebant.
chunks.english: And first indeed I wish to name the brothers of Fulda, because since I labored especially among them, writing many books that I handed over to our monastery, therefore among us also I wrote books that they themselves did not have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tradidi namque eis sicut memini, VII libellos, Herveldensibus autem duos libros.
chunks.english: For I handed over to them, as I remember, seven little books, but to those of Hersfeld two books.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cumque ex partibus illis remeassem, et ad Amarbach venissem, tradidi ejusdem loci abbati unum librum.
chunks.english: And when I had returned from those parts and come to Amorbach, I handed over one book to the abbot of that place.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Postea vero cum fratre nostro Wilhelmo profectus, dedi ei quatuor libros, inter quos erat Missalis liber satis pretiosus:
chunks.english: Later, however, having set out with our brother Wilhelm, I gave him four books, among which was a quite precious Missal.
chunks.notes: 4 books including valuable Missal to Wilhelm

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Abbati de Lauresheim unum librum; abbati de Ebersperg unum librum; amicis quibusdam in Boemia positis quatuor libros; amico cuidam in Patavia posito unum librum;
chunks.english: To the abbot of Lorsch one book; to the abbot of Ebersberg one book; to certain friends situated in Bohemia four books; to a certain friend situated in Passau one book.
chunks.notes: Lorsch (1) + Ebersberg (1) + Bohemia (4) + Passau (1) = 7 more books

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: ad monasterium Tegernsee duos libros; ad Pryelense coenobium prope nos positum volumen unum, in quo tres libri erant.
chunks.english: To the monastery of Tegernsee two books; to the monastery of Prüfening situated near us one volume, in which were three books.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed et filio sororis meae ibidem posito unum librum, variasque epistolas dedi.
chunks.english: But also to my sister's son situated there I gave one book and various letters.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad Superius ( Obermunster ) quoque monasterium tres libros; ad Inferius ( Nidernmunster ) monasterium unum librum dedi.
chunks.english: Also to the Upper Monastery three books; to the Lower Monastery I gave one book.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeterea multis aliis dedi aut misi aliquando sermones aut proverbia, seu aliqua aedificationis scripta.
chunks.english: Moreover, I gave or sent at some time to many others sermons or proverbs, or certain writings of edification.
chunks.notes: Additional uncounted distribution of smaller works

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Talia autem laboris mei indicia hic ideo protuli, ut aliquos monachos otiositati deditos converterem, et ad aliquod opus monasticae vitae congruum incitarem.
chunks.english: But I have brought forth such signs of my labor here so that I might convert some monks devoted to idleness and incite them to some work fitting for monastic life.
chunks.notes: Purpose: inspire lazy monks to productive work

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim tam magna nequeunt, faciliora agere possunt.
chunks.english: For if they cannot do such great things, they can do easier things.
